segments.s750-g409: This prepositional phrase z + noun phrase (ز بد دست ضحاک) comes first and marks source/agency—literally “from the bad hand of Zahhâk.” The verb ببست (past, 3rd sg.) follows the object تازی, so the clause order is: source/agent phrase — object — verb (“from Zahhâk’s evil hand an Arab he bound”). Note the terse, poetic omission of an explicit subject (it is understood from context).
segments.s750-g410: به مردی introduces an indirect-object-like phrase “to/for a man” (here marking the person acted on or affected), followed by a z‑phrase ز چنگ زمانه “from the clutch/grip of Time/Fortune.” The verb نجست (past, 3rd sg.) uses the sense “to leap/spring,” so the whole clause means that a man ‘leapt away’/‘sprang free’ from Time’s grip. Word order is prepositional phrase — verb, typical of epic Persian inversion.
segments.s750-g411: چو introduces a simile “like/as.” آرش is the compared name, and the relative particle که introduces the relative clause describing him. بردی is the past tense verb (here 'took/sent/used') and is followed by the measure phrase به فرسنگ تیر (“an arrow to/for a farsang”), so the clause reads “as Arash, who sent/carried an arrow to the distance of a farsang.” The compact, slightly elliptical order (verb near the middle) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s750-g412: A second simile introduced by چو (“like”), where پیروزگر is a descriptive epithet “victorious/triumphant,” followed by قارن (here used as a name/epithet) and شیرگیر as a compound noun ‘lion‑catcher’ (an epithet/occupational epithet). The noun phrase lacks an overt verb because it parallels the preceding clause: “like the triumphant Qâran, the lion‑catcher.”
segments.s750-g413: This is a name plus a relative clause: قباد followed by آنک (an archaic relative pronoun meaning ‘he who/that which’) and آمد ز البرز کوه ‘came from the Alborz mountain.’ The verb آمد is simple past ‘came,’ and the order places the relative clause immediately after the name, a common classical pattern.
segments.s750-g414: به مردی جهاندار شد is a compact predicate: به مردی here marks the domain/role ('among/into a man/hero'), جهاندار شد is a two-word verbal phrase where جهاندار is the predicate noun ‘world‑ruler/sovereign’ and شد (past of شدن) means ‘became.’ با گروه (‘with a band/company’) is an adjunct indicating accompaniment or means. Overall: ‘he became a sovereign among men with his company.’
segments.s750-g415: که introduces a relative clause ‘who.’ از آبگینه is an ablative phrase ‘from/out of glass’ (i.e., ‘of glass’), and همی + verb (همی ... کرد) is the classical imperfective/habitual marker—here rendering the action as a simple past narrative with a continuous flavor: ‘he made/constructed a house of glass.’ کرد is the main past tense verb ‘made/did.’
segments.s750-g416: وزان is a fused prepositional pronoun meaning ‘and/from that,’ linking back to the glass house; خانه is repeated for emphasis ‘that house.’ گیتی پر افسانه کرد is a verb phrase where پر (postpositive) means ‘full (of),’ افسانه is the object ‘legends/tales,’ and کرد is ‘made/filled.’ The clause thus reads: ‘and from that house he made the world full of tales.’
segments.s750-g113: درخشان is an adjective 'shining/brilliant' placed before the verb for poetic effect. شود is the present-subjunctive/optative form of شدن used here as a jussive/wish ('may become'). روزگار is the subject 'age/days/fortune' and بهی is an adjective 'good/excellent' describing روزگار; the clause literally reads 'may the good days become shining'.
segments.s750-g114: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/so that' introducing the result/purpose clause. تاج بزرگی is the noun phrase 'a great crown' (تاج = crown, بزرگی = greatness/large). به + سر forms the locative 'onto the head' and برنهی is a poetic/archaic subjunctive of the verb 'to place on' (بر نهی / بنَهی), here meaning 'you may put on' (the lemma given is misleading; functionally it's a 2nd-person subjunctive).
segments.s750-g115: مرا (here written split as م + را) is the first‑person object/experiencer 'to me' placed upfront for emphasis. آن زمان is the temporal adverbial 'at that time/then'. این سخن is 'this saying/remark'. بد درست is a compound adjectival phrase meaning 'ill‑fitting/unsuitable' (بد = bad/unfavourable, درست = proper/correct) and with no explicit copula it is predicative: 'this saying seemed improper to me.'
segments.s750-g116: ز is the poetic form of 'from'. دل مهربانی is a genitive phrase 'the heart of kindness' (دل = heart, مهربانی = kindness/mercy). نبایست is a negative deontic/modal 'ought not / should not' and شست is the verbal form of شستن 'to wash' (here passive/impersonal sense): 'ought not be washed/ought not to be wiped away.' The whole clause means 'it should not be washed away from a kind heart.'
segments.s750-g117: من آگاه بودم is a straightforward past statement 'I was aware.' که introduces the content clause 'that'. از بخت تو is a prepositional phrase 'by/through your fortune' (از = by/from, بخت = fortune/fate, تو = you), indicating the source or cause.
segments.s750-g118: ز marks instrument/source 'by/through'. کار is 'deed/work/act' and درخشیدن is the verb/infinitive 'to shine' used as a noun-like element meaning 'the shining' or 'glory' produced by action. تخت تو is 'your throne' (تخت = throne, تو = your). Together: 'that by your fortune / through your deeds your throne would shine.'
segments.s750-g119: نباشد here functions impersonally/negatively to mark lack/existence ('there will be not / I shall have no'). مرا again stands for 'for me/to me' (fronted possessive/experiencer). بهره means 'share/portion' and جز means 'except'. درد و رنج is a paired noun phrase 'pain and suffering.' The clause therefore means 'for me there will be no portion except pain and suffering.'
segments.s750-g120: تو را is the dative/benefactive 'to you' (marked with را for emphasis). گردد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of گشت/گردیدن used here to mean 'become / will fall to.' این تخت شاهی و گنج is the theme 'this royal throne and treasure' (تخت شاهی = royal throne, گنج = treasure). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: '[To] you will become this throne and treasure.'
segments.s750-g377: This clause uses the verb سپردیم (we entrusted) with the prepositional phrase به یزدان (to God) placed before it. چُــن here is a conjunction meaning 'when/after' (classical/poetic usage), and باز خواست functions as a verbal phrase meaning 'demanded back / reclaimed' (باز + خواست). Word order is Topic (to God) → Verb → subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s750-g378: This is a main clause with the negative epistemic verb ندانم (I do not know) + a nominal phrase as its object: زبان در دهانت (a tongue in your mouth). The suffix ت on دهانت marks 2nd person possession ('your'). چراست is a fused interrogative/explanatory predicate 'why is it (so)?' — together: 'I don't know why the tongue is in your mouth.'
segments.s750-g379: بگویم is a first-person present/subjunctive form 'I will say / I should say / I will speak' (used here as 'I will speak/tell'). The prepositional phrase به یزدان 'to God' is the indirect object; نه با کودکی negates that one would speak 'with a child' (i.e. 'not to a child').
segments.s750-g380: This is a relative clause modifying کودکی: که نشناسد او 'who does not know' — نشناسد is negative present subjunctive/indicative 'does not recognize/know.' The phrase بد ز نیک اندکی literally orders 'bad from good a little' (ز = 'from'); it means 'to distinguish bad from good even a little' (اندکی = 'a little / at all'). The whole clause: 'who cannot tell bad from good even a little.'
segments.s750-g381: The object phrase همه کار یزدان ('all the works/deeds of God') precedes the predicate پسندیده‌ام, which is perfect/passive-stative in form (past participle پسندیده + 1st-person singular suffix) meaning 'I have found/approved.' Word order (object → predicate) is typical for Persian poetic style.
segments.s750-g382: همان refers back to previously mentioned matters ('that same'); شور و تلخی is a paired nominal phrase 'passion and bitterness.' بسی (poetic form) intensifies or quantifies ('much' / 'a great deal'), and دیده‌ام is the perfect 'I have seen.' Together: 'I have seen that same passion and much bitterness.'
segments.s750-g383: مرا here is a dative/experiencer construction ('to me' / 'I had'), followed by بود 'was/had' and the noun phrase شاهی سی و هشت سال 'kingship / reign of thirty-eight years' indicating duration. The overall idiom means 'I held the kingship for thirty-eight years.'
segments.s750-g384: This clause states a lack of peers: کس از شهریاران نبودم همال literally 'no one among the kings was my equal.' کس is used in the negative sense 'no one'; از + شهریاران 'among the kings'; نبودم (I was not) + همال (equal/peer) — word order is inverted for poetic effect but yields 'I had no equal among rulers.'
segments.s750-g441: بسی is an intensifier here (classical/poetic 'many'/'much') placed before the noun. راه دشوار is a noun + adjective phrase (noun before adjective in Persian). بگذاشتیم is the past first-person plural of گذاشتن ('we placed/laid/caused'); here it takes راه دشوار as its object, meaning 'we set/laid (many) difficult ways'.
segments.s750-g442: بسی again intensifies 'many'. دشمن is the direct object 'enemies'. از پیش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from before/ahead (of us)'. برداشتیم is past first-person plural of برداشتَن 'we lifted/removed/took away' — here meaning 'we removed/cleared (them) from before us.'
segments.s750-g443: همه بومها = 'all the lands/regions' (همه as quantifier before the plural noun). پر ز گنج is the poetic construction 'full of (پر) + ز + noun' meaning 'full of treasure'. منست is an archaic fused copula here meaning 'is mine' (literally 'my is'), i.e. 'are full of my treasure.'
segments.s750-g444: کجا functions as an adverb 'where/wherever'. آب و خاکست is a compressed/poetic sequence = آب و خاک است ('there is water and earth'); note the copula است attached to خاک as -ست. رنج منست literally 'my suffering is' (رنج = suffering; منست = archaic 'is mine' / 'belongs to me'). Overall: 'Where there is water and earth, there is my toil.'
segments.s750-g445: چو here introduces a conditional 'if/when'. زین گونه = 'in this manner' (زین = 'from/this', گونه = 'manner, way'). بر من سرآید is the verbal phrase 'befalls me' (بر + indirect object + سرآید, subjunctive/present form meaning 'should come upon'). The clause ends with سرآید, and the main clause follows (جهان...).
segments.s750-g446: همی is an emphatic/continuous marker (classical 'will/still'). تیره گردد is 'becomes dark' (تیره = dark/ominous; گردد = present/subjunctive of گشتن used as 'become'). امید مهان = 'the hope of the guests' (noun + genitive/possession), so the full clause means 'the guests' hope will be darkened.'
segments.s750-g447: نماند is a negative existential/past form 'there remains not / will not remain' used impersonally. به فرزند من = 'for my son' (dative/beneficiary). نیز = 'also/too'. تخت stands as the affected noun 'throne' (object/topic). Together: 'No throne will remain for my son either.'
segments.s750-g448: بگردد ز تخت = '(he/it) will be turned away/from the throne' (بگردد = present/subjunctive of گشتن used 'become/turn'; ز تخت = 'from the throne'). و links the two verbs. سرآیدش is سرآید + enclitic -ش 'it/him befalls', meaning 'his fate will come upon him.' بخت = 'fortune/destiny.' The two verbs together express loss of throne and the coming of fate.
segments.s750-g121: ز here functions as a poetic preposition meaning 'out of/for the sake of' and introduces a causal list of abstract nouns joined by و; the nouns (بخشایش، دین، پیوند، مهر) enumerate motives or qualities (forgiveness, faith/obligation, tie/bond, affection) that set the attitude described in the next segment.
segments.s750-g122: نکردم is the first‑person past negative 'I did not make/do'; it takes دژم (an adjectival/predicative word 'sullen, grim') as its complement—poetically 'نکردم دژم' = 'I did not put on a sullen (or grim) look.' هیچ intensifies the negation ('at all/never'). زان نامه is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of that letter' and چهر ('face/appearance') names what would have been made sullen; overall the clause says the speaker never showed a gloomy face on account of that letter.
segments.s750-g123: به شیرین سپردم is a simple past transitive clause 'I entrusted/gave (it) to Shirin' (the direct object—likely the letter—is omitted but understood). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when', and برخواندم is a past tense verb 'I read aloud / recited' (here 'read it [aloud]' fits the context), so the time clause means 'when I read it.'
segments.s750-g124: ز here is the ablative 'from/of' and combines with هر گونه ('every kind of') to modify اندیشه‌ها ('thoughts, ideas'); راندم is the past active 'I drove away/dispelled'—so the clause states that the speaker drove away all kinds of thoughts (i.e. dismissed every thought). Note the plural suffix ها on اندیشه‌ها and the verb-final poetic order.
segments.s750-g125: بر اوست is a copular/existential phrase 'it rests upon him / he is'; ست is the copula attached after او. با اختر تو = 'with your star' (با + اختر + possessive تو). به هم is an adverbial phrase 'together/paired', so the whole phrase means 'he is (or his lot is) together with your star' — i.e. his destiny is linked to yours.
segments.s750-g126: نداند is third‑person present/past stem meaning 'knows' used here in a negative sense with the implied negation of the context (poetic word order places the verb before کسی 'anyone/no one'). زان سخن = 'of that speech/statement'; بیش و کم literally 'more and less' (= 'anything in detail' or 'more or less'). The clause says that no one knows (or can tell) anything more or less about that matter.
segments.s750-g127: گر introduces a conditional 'if.' ایدون is an archaism meaning 'now/at this time' (here functioning adverbially), then که خواهی 'that you wish' and the following که + بینی + بخواه form a purposive/imperative sequence: 'if now you wish to see, then ask' — بخواه here is an exhortation/imperative 'ask (for it, request it)'. The clause is elliptical and uses repeated که as conjunctions common in classical Persian.
segments.s750-g128: اگر at the start of the segment again gives a conditional 'if.' خود کنی is subjunctive/conditional 'you yourself do/make' (خود emphasizes the actor). بیش و کم 'more and less' are the items being altered; را is the definite object marker marking بیش و کم as the thing to be considered, and نگاه is used as 'take heed of / regard / look to' (a terse poetic command/infinitival sense). Overall: 'if you yourself change it (making it more or less), attend to those increases or decreases.'
segments.s750-g449: فرشته is the subject 'an angel' (a supernatural messenger). بیاید is an optative/potential present form ('may come' / 'should come') used in classical/poetic Persian. یکی جان ستان is a noun phrase appositive: یکی = 'someone/a certain one' and جان ستان is a compound meaning 'life‑stealer' (جان = 'soul, life' + ستان from the stem of ستاندن 'to take'); together they describe the expected visitor.
segments.s750-g450: بگویم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive (here hortative/future sense) 'I will say' or 'I shall tell'. بدو is the dative 'to him/to it'. جانم is the object 'my life/soul' (جان + possessive suffix م). آسان ستان is a short poetic command/phrase: آسان ('gently/easily') modifies ستان (root of ستاندن 'take'); in poetry the root form can function as an imperative/verb phrase meaning 'take (it) gently'.
segments.s750-g451: گذشتن here is a verbal noun/infinitive 'passing' or 'in passing'. چو = 'when / as'. بر introduces the location 'on/upon'. چینود appears as a poetic or difficult-to-classify element (likely a place or descriptive adverb) and is not straightforward here. پل بود is an existential clause 'there was a bridge', with بود as the past copula ('was').
segments.s750-g452: به introduces the locative phrase ('at / beneath'); زیر = 'under/beneath'; پی here means 'foot' (زیر پی = 'under the foot'). اندر = 'in/among'; همه گل = 'all/whole flowers' (i.e., 'flowers everywhere'). بود is the past copula 'there was'. The phrase describes the ground underfoot being full of flowers.
segments.s750-g453: به توبه دل is a prepositional phrase ‘by/with repentance of heart’ (به = 'by/with', توبه = 'repentance', دل = 'heart'). راست روشن کنیم is a hortative/first‑person plural construction: راست روشن = 'true/straight and bright' (adjectival/result phrase) and کنیم is the hortatory subjunctive 'let us make/let us render'. The verb کنیم governs the resulting state.
segments.s750-g454: بی‌آزاری خویش is a noun phrase 'our harmlessness' (بی‑آزاری = 'not harming / harmlessness'; خوی + ش = root 'disposition/one's nature' plus the possessive suffix ش = '—self/—own'). جوشن کنیم is 'let us make (into) a cuirass/armor' (جوشن = 'armor', کنیم = 'let us make'), i.e. ‘let us make our harmlessness into armor’. Note the split خوی + ش = خویش (our/one's own) in classical orthography.
segments.s750-g455: درستست is a fixed predicative phrase 'it is correct/true'. گفتار فرزانگان is the subject phrase 'the speech/sayings of the sages' (گفتار = 'speech', فرزانگان = 'sages'); note the poetic inversion where the predicate precedes the noun phrase it comments on.
segments.s750-g456: جهاندیده و پاک دانندگان are attributive epithets further describing the sages: جهاندیده = 'worldly‑wise/experienced', و = 'and', پاک = 'pure', دانندگان = 'those who know/knowers'. Grammatically these adjectives and the agent noun follow and qualify the earlier فرزانگان.
segments.s750-g49: به فرمان یزدان is an instrumental/prepositional phrase meaning 'by the command of God' (به here functions as 'by'). نیکی is the direct object ('goodness') placed before the verb in poetic word order. فزای is an archaic/poetic jussive/imperative form meaning 'cause to increase' / 'increase!' (from the root فزودن/فزاییدن).
segments.s750-g50: که introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for' / 'since' / 'that'). اویست here is the contracted copula + pronoun (او + است = 'he/it is') used poetically. بر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase 'with regard to / over good and evil' and رهنمای is a predicate noun 'guide' completing the copula ('He is the guide concerning good and evil').
segments.s750-g51: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ایران و توران is the compound subject. به آرام گشت is a verbal phrase where به + adjective (آرام) expresses a change of state ('into calm/peace'), and گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became'. The adjective phrase precedes the verb in customary Persian order.
segments.s750-g52: همه کار is a noun phrase 'all (the) affairs' which, with the following proper name بهرام, reads as 'all of Bahram's affairs' (possessive relation without an explicit ezafe in poetic diction). ناکام گشت is the predicate: ناکام (unsuccessful/thwarted) + گشت (became) — 'became unsuccessful'.
segments.s750-g53: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. از جنگ چوبینه is a prepositional phrase 'from the wooden battle' (چوبینه = 'wooden' / 'of clubs', describing the battle). پرداختم is 1st-person singular past; in epic/poetic usage پرداختن can mean 'to set out' / 'to sally forth' here ('I went forth from the battle').
segments.s750-g54: نخستین is an adverb/ordinal 'first'. به کین پدر is a purpose/benefactive phrase 'for (the) vengeance of (my) father' (به = 'for/to' here). تاختم is the 1st-person past of تاختن 'I charged/attacked' (here meaning 'I attacked/rode to take vengeance'). Word order places the adverb and purpose phrase before the verb, typical of classical poetic style.
segments.s750-g55: چو = 'when'. بندوی و گستهم appear as two coordinated referents (likely proper names in this epic context). خالان is the plural noun 'moles / beauty-spots' placed before the verb in poetic order, and بدند is the past 3rd-person plural of بودن ('they were'); together this yields 'when Banduy and Gostaham were/appeared with moles' (i.e. marked with beauty-spots).
segments.s750-g56: به هر کشوری = 'in every country/realm' (به used locatively), بی‌همالان is a predicate adjective/noun meaning 'without peers / peerless ones', and بدند again is 'they were' (past 3rd plural). The phrase means 'they were peerless in every land.'
segments.s750-g473: برفتند is the past-tense verb (3rd person plural) meaning “they departed.” گریان is an adverbial/participial adjective “weeping,” describing the manner of their departure. ز پیشش به در is a series of prepositional phrases: ز + پیشش = “from before him / from his presence,” and به در = “toward the door,” showing origin and direction of movement.
segments.s750-g474: پر از درد جان is a predicative phrase meaning “full of the pain of the soul” (پر = “full (of)” + از = “of/from” + درد جان = “soul’s pain”). و links the next descriptor: پراندوه سر is a compound adjective+noun: پراندوه = “full of sorrow” modifying سر = “head,” i.e. “heads full of sorrow.” The whole segment gives their emotional state.
segments.s750-g475: به نزدیک شیرویه is a locative phrase “to/near Shiruyah” (به + نزدیک + proper name). رفت is the past tense verb “went”; in classical/poetic Persian the verb can appear before the subject for emphasis, so رفت این دو مرد = “these two men went,” with the subject following the verb.
segments.s750-g476: پر آژنگ رخسار is a descriptive phrase: پر = “full (of),” آژنگ is a descriptive noun modifying رخسار (countenance/face) so the phrase means “faces full of آژنگ” (here: a kind of color/complexion). و links the next clause: دل پر ز درد = “heart full of pain” (دل = heart; پر = full; ز = of/from; درد = pain). The two coordinated descriptions give appearance and inner feeling.
segments.s750-g477: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one / each in turn.” بدادند is past tense, 3rd person plural of دادن, “they gave/delivered.” پیغام شاه is the possessed noun phrase “the king’s message” (noun + possessor following it). The whole segment states the action and its object.
segments.s750-g478: به marks the recipient (“to Shiruyah”); شیروی is the addressee (here a proper name/title). بی‌مغز and بی‌دستگاه are coordinating negative/adjectival compounds applied to that addressee: بی‌مغز = “without brain / brainless” (figuratively: foolish), بی‌دستگاه = “without device/organization” i.e. “without means or support.” و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s750-g433: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). اندر + noun is an older locative preposition meaning 'in' or 'among'. این ازان is an inverted/poetic order meaning 'these of them' (i.e. 'these men'); به بدی is a nominal phrase 'in badness' = 'worse' (به + abstract noun). The whole segment says: 'for in the skill/virtue of these there was badness/worse-ness.'
segments.s750-g434: به سال is an adverbial measure 'by a year' (به + سال = 'by a year's [difference]'). آن یکی از دگر is a compact poetic expression 'that one from the other' = 'one (of them) compared to the other'. مه بدی uses مه in the sense 'great/much' modifying بدی ('badness'), so together it stresses the degree of difference. The segment therefore yields: 'by a year's [difference] one was worse than the other.'
segments.s750-g435: بپرداختند is a past tense plural verb 'they attended to / handled' (poetic prefix be- + پرداختند). این جهان فراخ is the direct object phrase 'this broad/spacious world' (فراخ = 'spacious'). Word order is typical for elevated/poetic Persian: verb then object phrase for emphasis.
segments.s750-g436: بماندند is past plural 'they remained / were left' (بر + ماندند in older style). The following nouns میدان و ایوان و کاخ are listed things that 'remained' — 'the arena(s), iwan/porch, and palace(s)'. The verb governs the series of nouns that follow.
segments.s750-g437: ز شاهان is an archaic/genitive-like phrase 'of/from the kings' meaning 'among the kings'. مرا (split in the word list into 'م' + 'را') functions as the emphasized first-person object/topic 'me / I' (را marks the topicalized/definite element). نیز 'also' modifies the clause; همتا نبود is the predicate 'there was no peer/equal'. The whole means 'among kings I too had no equal.'
segments.s750-g438: اگر at the start sets a counterfactual/exception 'if (it were not that)'. سال را چند is an idiomatic quantifying phrase 'a few years' (سال with را for emphasis + چند 'several/a few'). بالا here means 'high/advanced (in age)' or 'considerable'; نبود is past negative 'was not'. Together the clause supplies the qualification: 'if it were not for some years (difference).'
segments.s750-g439: جهان را سپردم is simple past first-person 'I entrusted/handed over the world' (را marks جهان as definite/accusative). به نیک و به بد is a parallel dative phrase 'to good and to bad' meaning 'to good and evil/fortune and misfortune'. Together: 'I left the world to good and to ill.'
segments.s750-g440: نه ... که introduces a negative clause 'not that ...' (negating a particular reason). آن را is a demonstrative object 'that (thing)', که introduces the subordinate content clause. روزی here functions adverbially 'one day / someday'; به من بد رسد is an impersonal construction 'bad (misfortune) come to me' (رسد = 'befall / happen'). The clause denies that the speaker's resignation was because some misfortune would someday befall him.
segments.s750-g401: بمیرد is an older/poetic present-subjunctive/optative form (ب + میرد) meaning ‘dies’ / ‘may die’ or ‘will die’; it stands before کسی (indefinite 'someone') because Classical Persian often places the verb before the subject. کو here is an archaic/poetic relative particle (= 'who') linking کسی to the relative clause; ز مادر بزاد is a short relative phrase 'born of a mother' with ز (poetic 'from/of') + مادر and the perfective/becoming verb بزاد ('is born'). The whole clause states a general truth: 'whoever is born of a mother dies.'
segments.s750-g402: This is a temporal-range phrase introduced by ز ('from' in poetic register) marking the span of persons/events referred to: 'from Kay Khosrow's beginning (ز کیخسرو آغاز) تا ('up to/until') کیقباد.' آغاز is a noun 'beginning' and تا links it to the endpoint کیقباد; there is no finite verb here because this line continues the general statement about mortality across that period.
segments.s750-g403: چو here functions as a comparative/temporal particle 'as' or 'when' and introduces the list of names (هوشنگ و طهمورث و جمشید). The coordination uses و as the ordinary 'and'; the sequence names legendary kings who serve as the exemplar for what follows.
segments.s750-g404: کزیشان is a fused prepositional/relative phrase: کز (poetic from/of) + ایشان ('them'), here = 'from them' or 'by them.' بدی is the noun 'evil'; جای is 'place/realm' and governs the following nouns بیم وامید ('fear and hope'). Grammatically this clause implies that, by/through these kings, evil was removed (context supplies the verb) from the sphere/seat of fear and hope — a compact, somewhat elliptical classical construction.
segments.s750-g405: که here introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'); the nouns دیو و دد و دام are listed with و as 'and' (demon, foe/enemy, and trap). فرمانش is the noun 'his command' with the enclitic -ش (his). برد is an older/poetic perfect or preterite of بردن ('took' / 'carried off') used transitively — the clause literally says 'demon, enemy, and trap took his command.'
segments.s750-g406: چو = 'when'; روشن سرآمد is a nominal phrase 'his bright/clear end' (روشن 'bright/clear' modifying سرآمد 'end/conclusion'); برفت is an archaic/past verb meaning 'went away / departed' (classical form of رفت), followed by و (and) and بمرد, a classical perfect/optative form meaning 'he died' or 'and he died.' Together the clause gives the temporal consequence: 'when his bright end arrived, he departed and died.'
segments.s750-g407: This is a nominative headline: فریدون فرخ ('Fereydun the Fortunate') followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('who') and the pronoun او ('he'). از جهان begins a prepositional phrase 'from the world' that will be completed by the verb in the next segment; word order places the subject and relative before that verb in typical classical style.
segments.s750-g408: بدی دور کرد is a compact verbal clause: بدی 'evil' is the object, دور کرد is a light-verb construction where دور (literally 'away/remote') conveys 'remove' and کرد (from کردن) is the causative/do verb — together 'caused/removed (evil) away.' آشکار و نهان are a paired adverb/adjective 'openly and secretly' (literally 'manifest and hidden'), modifying the action to mean he removed evil both overtly and covertly.
segments.s750-g1: بدان functions as a compound of the prepositional prefix بَ- + anaphoric/ demonstrative (roughly 'to that' or 'unto that'), introducing the indirect object ('to that famed one'). نامور is the noun 'famed/renowned' in vocative/accusative position. گفت is the (archaic/poetic) past tense 'said' introducing reported speech, and پاسخ شنو is the imperative phrase 'hear (the) answer' with پاسخ 'answer' as its object and شنو the imperative 'listen/hear'. The sequence shows: he said to that famed one: “answer — listen.”
segments.s750-g2: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. ببر here is the imperative of بردن ('take'/'carry'/'bring'), not the animal. سوی is the directional preposition 'toward/to', سالار is 'commander/chief', and نو is an adjective 'new'. Word order is verb (imperative) + direction + indirect object, i.e. 'Take/bring them one by one to the new commander.'
segments.s750-g3: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + ضمیرِ متصل -یش = 'to him'). که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content clause. زشت کسان را مجوی is a negative imperative clause: زشت 'vile' qualifies کسان 'people', را marks them as definite direct object, and مجوی is the negative imperative 'do not seek'. Together: 'Tell him that he should not seek vile people.'
segments.s750-g4: جز آن را که is a restrictive phrase 'except that which...', where آن (with object marker را) is the thing excepted and که introduces a relative clause. برتابی is a verb/adjective 'that you can bear/ endure' (second person singular potential), followed by از ننگ روی 'of shame on the face' (از = 'of/from', ننگ = 'shame', روی = 'face'). Overall: 'except that which you can endure of shame on your face' (i.e. only what you can bear without disgrace).
segments.s750-g5: سخن هرچ گفتی is a noun phrase: سخن 'words/speech' modified by the relative/indefinite هرچ گفتی 'whatever you said' (هرچ = 'whatever', گفتی = 'you said'). نه introduces negation of the following predicative phrase. گفتارتست is a fused classical construction equivalent to گفتارت است 'it is your speech'; with نه it yields 'it is not your speech' or 'not truly your words.'
segments.s750-g6: مماناد is an archaic/poetic negative perfect/past form meaning 'did not remain' or 'do not let remain' (contextually an admonition). گویا here qualifies زبانت ('your tongue/speech') — either 'clear/eloquent' or 'manifest' — and درست is an adjective 'correct/proper'. The clause urges: 'Do not let them remain; make your speech/ tongue clear and correct.'
segments.s750-g7: مگو is the negative imperative 'do not say'. آنچ is a relative 'that which/what'. بدخواه تو is a genitive phrase 'your ill-wisher/enemy' (بدخواه = hostile person; تو = your). بشنود is the (present/subjunctive) third-person form 'may hear' or 'might hear' (classical optative/subjunctive), making the clause mean 'do not say what your enemy may hear.'
segments.s750-g8: ز گفتار بیهوده is a prepositional phrase 'from/from the effect of vain/pointless speech' (ز = poetic 'from/of', گفتار = 'speech', بیهوده = 'in vain/pointless'). شادان شود is a verb phrase 'he becomes glad/joyful' (شود = 'become', شادان = 'glad'), describing the undesired result of the enemy hearing such words.
segments.s750-g305: چنان دان is an imperative/imperatival instruction meaning 'know this' or 'consider thus' (دان here = 'know/consider'). که introduces a content clause ('that' / 'who'), and نوشین روان قباد is an epithet phrase: نوشین (adjective) modifies روان ('soul/spirit'), followed by the name قباد — i.e. 'Qobad of sweet/pleasant spirit.'
segments.s750-g306: به اندرز این کرد is built around the main verb کرد ('made/did') with به اندرز این = 'this into counsel' or 'made this counsel'; به marks the transformation/assignment. در نامه یاد is a locative/prepositional phrase ('as a reminder in the letter'), where یاد (a noun) is the result/role assigned by کرد.
segments.s750-g307: که introduces the clause ('that/for'); هرکو is the archaic fused form of هر که = 'whoever' (indefinite subject). سلیحش is 'his weapon' (سلیح + enclitic -ش). به دشمن دهد is a straightforward transitive clause 'gives to the enemy' with دهد in the present/generalive sense ('gives' / general truth).
segments.s750-g308: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('thus/indeed' here). خویشتن را is the emphatic object 'himself' with را as the definite object marker. به کشتن دهد uses the verbal noun کشتن ('killing') with به to mark the direction/result — 'gives (himself) to killing' i.e. 'hands himself over to be killed.' دهد remains the main verb.
segments.s750-g309: که چون = 'for when/as' introducing a temporal clause; بازخواهد is a future-oriented compound meaning 'will again' or 'will return to' (باز + خواهد). کش آید is a verbal sequence where کش (related to 'pull/draw', here 'draw [a sword]') combines with آید ('comes') to mean 'draws and comes' or 'comes carrying/drawing [a weapon]'. به کار = 'into action/affair' (the arena of deeds).
segments.s750-g310: بداندیش is a substantive/adjective 'the ill‑minded one' functioning as subject; با او کند کارزار is the predicate: با 'with', او 'him', کند (present/habitual third‑person form of کردن) 'does/makes' and کارزار 'battle' — together 'will wage battle against him.' کند is used here as the verb expressing the action.
segments.s750-g311: دگر آنک = 'the other (thing) which' (دگر = 'other/another', آنک = relative 'which/that'). دادی is the past tense 'you gave' and ز قیصر پیام literally 'a message from Caesar' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and پیام is the noun 'message/tidings.' The whole phrase names the alternate matter previously mentioned.
segments.s750-g312: مرا (here split as م + را in the word list) is the accusative 'me' (را marks the object). خواندی is second‑person past 'you called/named' — here used in the sense 'you named/called me (such)'. دو دل 'two‑hearted' means 'double‑minded/fickle', و links, and خویش کام is a compound meaning 'self‑seeking/self‑willed' (خوی = disposition/habit, ش = possessive clitic, کام = desire).
segments.s750-g465: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. اشتاد و خراد are two names/epithets joined by و 'and'. برزین گو functions here as a name/epithet attached to (one of) the persons (the compound Burzin‑go) rather than a verb; گو is not acting as 'say' but as part of the name/epithet. Word order is typical: the temporal clause starts with چو and the persons follow.
segments.s750-g466: شنیدند is 3rd person plural past 'they heard'. پیغام آن پیش رو functions as a noun phrase 'the message of that forerunner/messenger' — پیش رو here is a compound meaning 'forerunner/messenger'. The demonstrative آن 'that' modifies پیش رو. The verb comes at the end in normal Persian past‑tense word order.
segments.s750-g467: به introduces an instrumental/metaphorical phrase 'with' or 'by': پیکان دل 'the arrow of the heart' is a metaphorical instrument of grief or pain. هر دو دانا 'each of the two wise ones' is the subject phrase. بخست is a past‑tense verb (3rd person plural) — literal sense listed is 'shut/closed', but in this poetic context it functions as a past action of wounding/piercing the heart (image of inflicting grief). The verb follows its subject as typical in Persian past clauses.
segments.s750-g468: به سر بر زدند is a verbal phrase meaning 'they struck at (their) heads' — به + سر marks the target (at the head) and زدند is the 3rd person plural past 'they struck'. آن زمان 'that moment' is a temporal phrase inserted after the verb; هر دو دست 'with both hands' gives the instrument. Note literal word order places the verb before the temporal phrase but the sense remains 'at that moment they struck their heads with both hands.'
segments.s750-g469: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/ because of' introducing the cause: گفتار 'speech/words'. هر دو refers back to 'both (of them)'. پشیمان شدند is a compound: پشیمان 'regretful' + شدند (3rd person plural past of شدن) 'they became' — together 'they became regretful'. The predicate follows the causal prepositional phrase.
segments.s750-g470: به marks the target/instrumental relation 'on/with': رخسارگان (plural of رخسار) = 'faces/cheeks' is the affected body part. بر + تپنچه زدند = 'they struck with the palm' (تپنچه here denotes the flat of the hand/palm in this poetic usage; زدند is 3rd person plural past 'they struck'). The construction is cause/result: being regretful, they beat/slapped their faces.
segments.s750-g471: به ... بر ... is a prepositional/locative frame emphasizing location 'on/upon'. همه جامشان چاک بود = 'all (of) their garments were torn' — همه 'all' modifies جامشان (their garments/robes), چاک 'a rent or gash', and بود is the past copula 'was/were'. In context جامشان refers to their clothing (poetic usage) though the lemma given can also mean 'cup'; the adjective/noun چاک describes the state.
segments.s750-g472: سر هر دو دانا 'the heads of both wise men' is the subject; پر از خاک بود is a predicative phrase 'were full of dust' where پر (here 'full (of)') + از ('of') + خاک ('dust/earth') and بود 'was/were'. The whole clause states that their heads were covered or filled with dust (i.e., dusty or soiled).
segments.s750-g425: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction) introducing a subordinate clause. گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz'. و here is the coordinating 'and'. هفتاد پور گزین literally 'seventy sons chosen' where پور = 'sons' and گزین = 'chosen/selected' (adjectival); the order is noun-number-noun-adjective, a common poetic compression meaning 'his seventy chosen sons'.
segments.s750-g426: سواران میدان = 'the horsemen of the field/arena' (سواران 'horsemen', میدان 'field/arena'); و = 'and' links the two coordinated NPs; شیران کین = 'lions of vengeance' where شیران is literal/metaphorical 'lions' (brave fighters) and کین is a genitive attributive 'of vengeance/hatred'. The whole segment apposes/describes the seventy sons as chosen warriors.
segments.s750-g427: چو = 'when' (temporal). گشتاسپ is the proper name 'Gushtasp'. شاهی here = 'kingship' or 'being king' (abstract noun), so 'Gushtasp's accession to kingship' is being narrated. که introduces the relative/subordinate clause linking to what follows. دین بهی = 'the Good Religion' (دین 'religion' + بهی 'good/ excellent'), a fixed epithet for the (Zoroastrian) faith.
segments.s750-g428: پذیرفت = past tense 'accepted' (he accepted the religion). و = 'and'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'از/from' (often written ز or زِ) used idiomatically as 'thereby / from that'. The following و in the segmentation appears as a separate token but functions as part of the clausal flow (in the manuscript the sense is 'از او' = 'from him/thereby'); تازه شد فرهی = 'became newly/freshly noble' (تازه 'new/fresh', شد past of شدن 'became', فرهی 'noble/cultured'). Overall: 'he accepted and thereby became newly noble.'
segments.s750-g429: چو = 'when'. جاماسپ is the proper name 'Jamasp'. کاندر (contraction of 'کانه در' / 'کان در') = 'in which / among' (poetic locative); شمار = 'number, rank'; سپهر = 'the sky/heavens'. Together they form a locative phrase 'among the ranks (or in the reckoning) of the heavens', a poetic way to place Jamasp high in celestial order.
segments.s750-g430: فروزنده‌تر = comparative 'more radiant/bright' (فروزنده 'radiant' + comparative suffix -تر). بد here is an archaic poetic copula corresponding to 'بود' (i.e. 'was'); it is not the ordinary adjective 'bad' in this line. ز = poetic preposition 'than/from' introducing the standard of comparison. گردنده مهر = 'the revolving/turning مهر' where گردنده 'rotating' and مهر refers to the divinity/sun (Mithra) — so 'more radiant than the revolving Sun/Mithra.'
segments.s750-g431: شدند = past plural 'they became' (شد + plural ending -ند). آن = the demonstrative 'those/the'. بزرگان = 'the great ones' (plural of بزرگ). و = 'and' linking coordinated nouns. دانندگان = 'knowers/learned ones' (plural), functioning as a coordinated descriptor of the same people.
segments.s750-g432: سواران جنگی = 'war-horsemen' or 'mounted warriors' (سواران 'horsemen' + جنگی 'warlike/military' as an attributive adjective). و = 'and'. مردانگان = plural noun meaning 'men' with a valorous sense — here 'men of valor/heroic men'. This segment gives the new roles/qualities those great and learned ones assumed.
segments.s750-g369: برفتی is a past-tense verb placed at the start for poetic emphasis (literally “you departed/vanished”). خود is emphatic/reflexive here, reinforcing the subject ('himself/you yourself'). از گنج ما is a prepositional phrase 'from our treasure/treasury' that follows the verb, and ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' placed after the phrase for added adverbial emphasis.
segments.s750-g370: مسیحا is a predicative noun 'Messiah' placed before the copular/auxiliary شد ('became'); شد is the past 'became' and the following او is the third-person pronoun. نیستی is used as a nominal/abstract idea of non‑existence (or poetic negative state) with در جهان specifying location/realm 'in the world' — the whole clause says he has become a messianic figure and is no longer present in the world.
segments.s750-g371: دگر works as 'the other/again' pointing to another matter; آنک + گفتی is a relative construction 'that which you said' (آنک = 'that which'). که introduces the subordinate clause commanding the action, and پوزش بگوی is an instructive phrase where پوزش 'apology' is the object and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive-like verb 'say' (older poetic form), i.e. 'say/offer apology.'
segments.s750-g372: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; توبه کن is an imperative built from the noun توبه 'repentance' plus the light verb کن 'do' yielding 'repent!'; راه یزدان بجوی is another imperative clause 'seek the way of God' where راه یزدان 'the way/path of God' is the object and بجوی is the second‑person imperative/optative 'seek'.
segments.s750-g373: ورا is a dative/locative pronoun 'to him' (or 'there/to that'); پاسخ آن بد is a noun phrase 'that bad/harsh answer.' که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; ریزنده is a present‑participle/adjectival agent 'scattering' or 'that scatters', and باد here functions as an optative/auxiliary ('may it be' / 'would be') giving a wish or potential force to the relative clause.
segments.s750-g374: This is a coordinated list of body terms 'زبان و دل و دست و پای قباد' ('tongue and heart and hand and foot of Qobad') functioning as the objects affected by the preceding participle 'scattering.' The possessive relationship is signalled by placing قباد after پای, so the name Qobad applies to all listed members.
segments.s750-g375: مرا here is the accusative 'me' (م + را, object marker) indicating the recipient; تاج یزدان is a genitive construction 'the crown of God'; به سر is the locative 'on the head'; and برنهاد is a past tense verb 'placed on' (literally 'set upon'), so the clause reads 'He set God's crown upon my head.'
segments.s750-g376: پذیرفتم is the past first‑person 'I accepted' (I received/admitted); و is the coordinating 'and'; بودم is the past 'I was'; از followed by a noun expresses cause/relation 'because of/from the crown' (از تاج) and شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy' — together: 'I accepted it and was happy because of the crown.'
segments.s750-g65: به فرمان ما is an agent phrase where به + noun is used in the sense 'by' (i.e. 'by our command'). ناگهان is an adverb placed before the verb to mean 'suddenly'. کشته شد is a passive construction: کشته (past participle 'killed') + شد (auxiliary 'be') = 'was killed'.
segments.s750-g66: سر و رای is a coordinated noun phrase ('head and counsel') meaning leaders/plans; the conjunction و joins two nouns. خونخوارگان is a plural agent noun 'the blood‑thirsty'. گشته شد is resultative/passive: گشته (past participle 'become/turned') + شد (auxiliary) yields 'were made/overturned' or 'came to be ended'.
segments.s750-g67: دگر here is a discourse particle meaning 'the other' / 'as for the other (matter)'. آنک is a relative 'that which / what', and گفتی (here a nominalized sense 'what was said' / 'saying') with the emphatic pronoun تو refers back to 'what you said'. از کار خویش is a prepositional phrase 'concerning your affairs' where کار خوی + ش marks 'your own affairs'.
segments.s750-g68: از آن here continues 'about/from that'; تنگ زندان و بازار خویش is a coordinated noun phrase 'that narrow prison and (your) market', with خوی + ش supplying the possessive 'your'. There is no finite verb in this segment — it continues and specifies the matter mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s750-g69: بد آن is an evaluative phrase 'bad is that' (adjective before demonstrative). تا functions here as a subordinating conjunction introducing the clause 'that ...'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', so ز فرزند من کار بد literally 'a bad deed from my son.' کار بد is 'bad deed' (کار used as 'deed/act').
segments.s750-g70: نیاید is a negative optative/subjunctive meaning 'may not come / should not happen'. کزان is a relative 'from which'; it links to the following clause. بر سرش بد رسد is a fixed expression 'bad befalls upon his head' where بر سرش ('upon his head') precedes the verb رسد, and رسد is used in the sense 'befall/happen'.
segments.s750-g71: به زندان نبد uses نبد (classical negative form) to express non‑existence: 'there was/is no prison'. به here marks the domain ('as for / regarding prison'). بر شما تنگ و بند is a predicative phrase 'narrow and fettering upon you' (تنگ 'narrow/tight' and بند 'fetter/bond'), describing what is absent; the clause expresses a wish/negation that you be confined.
segments.s750-g72: همان is a demonstrative 'that same' introducing an appositive noun group. زخم خواری و بیم گزند is a coordinated nominal phrase 'wound (injury), humiliation and fear of harm' that names the misfortunes referred to; there is no verb because this noun phrase elaborates what is to be avoided or absent.
segments.s750-g73: 'بدان' here is the poetic contraction of 'به آن' meaning 'on that'; 'روزتان' is 'روز' + possessive 'تان' ('your day'). 'خوار نگذاشتم' is a negated transitive past: 'نگذاشتم' (I did not allow/let) plus the predicate adjective 'خوار' ('humiliated/despised'), with the sufferer (you) implied; literal order is 'on-that your-day humiliated I-did-not-let'.
segments.s750-g74: Word order is object/adjunct before the verb: 'همه گنج' (all the treasure) + locative/benefactive 'پیش شما' ('in your presence / before you') followed by the past verb 'داشتم' ('I had'). This expresses possession in the past: 'I had all the treasure before you.'
segments.s750-g75: 'بر آیین شاهان پیشین' is a prepositional phrase 'according to the custom/practice of former kings.' The final 'بدیم' is an archaic/poetic past form (cf. classical 'دادیم') meaning 'we gave/did/observed' and is used here to mean 'we acted/behaved (in that way)'; the subject is plural (poetic or royal plural).
segments.s750-g76: 'نه' negates the following adjective 'بی‌کار' ('not idle'). The conjunction 'و' links to another clause 'بر دیگر آیین بدیم' which repeats the prepositional pattern 'according to another custom we acted.' Again 'بدیم' is the archaic past form meaning 'we acted/gave/observed.'
segments.s750-g77: The opening 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from.' The phrase strings several sources together: 'نخچیر' (booty/spoil), then the poetic contraction 'وز' (= 'و ز', 'and from'), then 'گوی' (here 'speaker/words/poet') and 'رامشگران' (plural agent noun 'peacemakers' or 'peacemakers/comforters'). The line lists items/sources using poetic conjunctions.
segments.s750-g78: 'ز کاری' begins another 'from' + noun phrase ('from deeds/work'). 'که' is a relative pronoun 'that/which'; 'اندر' is the locative 'in/among.' The final phrase 'خور مهتران' is compact/archaic: 'خور' here is related to 'eating' or what is consumed (poetically 'the portion/feast') and 'مهتران' refers to chiefs/elders; overall: 'from deeds that are among the chiefs' (i.e., deeds appropriate to great men).
segments.s750-g79: شمارا (you with the object marker -را) is fronted for emphasis; به چیزی is a prepositional phrase 'of/for anything'; نبودی is the past negative form 'was not / had not' and links to شمارا as the experiencer; نیاز is the predicate noun 'need' (literal: 'For you there was not need of anything').
segments.s750-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing a list governed by that preposition; the sequence lists the things one might desire. The token وز here functions as the contracted combination وِ ز (and of) in classical/poetic usage (not the literal 'gust' or 'weight'), and the remaining items are simple nouns joined by و ('and').
segments.s750-g337: بگوید is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of گفتن ('to say') and here introduces reported speech addressed to the second person. تو را uses the object/definite marker را after the pronoun (object = “you”). زاد here is a noun 'birth/origin' (the second sense) and is modified by فرخ ('fortunate' as an epithet). همین is a demonstrative ‘this/this very’, identifying the particular birth/origin being spoken of.
segments.s750-g338: جهان را is the (definite) object/topic 'the world' with the object marker را. به here has the instrumental/‘by/with’ sense (used to mark the manner: 'with/by the eye'). چشم جوانی is an oblique noun phrase 'the eye/sight of youth' (چشم = sight/vision here). مبین in this position functions as an imperative/optative or poetic verb-form meaning 'see/consider' (archaic usage); its form is not the modern بَبین but a classical poetic form.
segments.s750-g339: گشسپ is a proper name. آنک is a relative particle 'who/that' introducing the clause describing him. بد here is uncertain/archaic in this context (it can mean 'bad' or be part of an older verbal form) and does not fit the listed nominal senses directly. نیز means 'also/likewise', and گنجور is an adjective/noun meaning 'treasure-laden' or 'treasury' qualifying him (i.e. 'rich in treasure' or 'treasury-man'). ما is the possessive/topic 'our'.
segments.s750-g340: همان is a demonstrative 'the same' identifying the person. موبد پاک is a noun phrase 'pure mōbed' (adjective before noun, as usual). دستور ما is a genitive construction 'our order/discipline' where ما after دستور marks possession ('of our order'). The whole segment names/identifies Goshasp as 'that same pure priest of our order.'
segments.s750-g341: که is the relative/conjunction 'who/that' linking this clause to the person just named. از گنج ما literally 'from our treasure/treasury' (از marks origin/source). بدره is a poetic noun (literally 'full moon') used as a metaphor for a round/beautiful jewel; here it names the jewel taken. The final بد in the listed words is likely an archaic/metrical form (or a damaged reading) and does not match the provided senses directly.
segments.s750-g342: که ... دادم begins a subordinate clause 'which I gave': که is 'which/that', دادم is the 1st-person past 'I gave'. The sequence ب + دان in the manuscript here functions as an archaic unit corresponding to 'to/into/that' (ب = preposition 'to', دان part of an older compound) and introduces the indirect object. رومیان is the indirect object 'to the Romans', and یادگار is a noun meaning 'memento/keepsake' placed after the verb to indicate the purpose/role of what was given.
segments.s750-g343: نیاطوس is a proper name (the recipient). را is the definite/object marker attached to the name (Niatous = direct/indirect object). مهره is the noun 'bead/amulet' (here 'beads' or gem-beads) and دادم is the 1st-person past 'I gave' (verb governing the object and quantity). The numeral 'thousand' (هزار) follows or accompanies دادم in the line as the quantity given.
segments.s750-g344: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'. یاقوت سرخ is a genitive/attributive phrase 'red ruby' (یاقوت = ruby, سرخ = red). از ... در گوشوار reads as a locative/source phrase 'from/in the earring' (در = 'in/inside' and گوشوار = 'earring/ornament'); together they indicate these beads came out of the earring's setting. Word order follows classical Persian: preposition + noun + adjective, then a locative phrase.
segments.s750-g265: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/against' introducing the source of the feeling; یزدان پرستنده is a genitive phrase 'the worshipper of God'. بیزار گشت is a two-word predicate where بیزار is a predicate adjective 'averse/disgusted' and گشت is the past tense/resultative verb 'became'. The clause therefore reads 'the worshipper of God became averse.'
segments.s750-g266: ورا is a dative/benefactive pronoun 'for/to him' (classical order before the rest); نام و آواز تو is a coordinated noun phrase 'your name and (your) renown/voice' where آواز functions idiomatically as 'reputation'. خوار گشت repeats the same copular pattern: خوار (despised/worthless) + گشت 'became' — 'became despised.'
segments.s750-g267: چو is the conditional 'if/when'; بی‌گنج is an adjectival phrase 'without treasure' modifying the implied subject تو. باشی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/copular form 'you are' (used in the protasis). نپاید is a negative present/resultative verb 'will not last / will not stand', and سپاه is the (postposed, poetic) subject 'the army' — literally 'if you are treasureless, the army will not endure.'
segments.s750-g268: تو را uses تو plus the object/contrast marker را to mark 'you' as the patient/object of the following verb; زیردستان is the plural subject 'the subordinates/vassals'. نخوانند is a negative present/optative form 'they will not call/name', here meaning 'will not recognize as'; شاه is the predicative complement 'king'. Word order is topical/object before the verb (poetic inversion): 'your subordinates will not call you king.'
segments.s750-g269: سگ آن به که is an idiomatic comparative construction 'that dog is better which...' where آن به که introduces the relative clause. خواهنده نان بود is the relative clause 'which was a beggar for bread' (خواهنده 'one who desires/asks', نان 'bread', بود past copula 'was'). The whole segment expresses a proverblike comparison: 'Better that dog which begs for bread.'
segments.s750-g270: چو is here 'when/if'; سیرش is the object with possessive suffix 'its/ him' meaning 'its satiety' or 'when you satiate it'; کنی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/present of کردن 'to make/do' — 'when you satiate it'. دشمن جان بود is the result clause 'it was/ became a deadly enemy' (دشمن جان = mortal enemy; بود past copula). The structure is protasis followed by apodosis: 'if/when you fill it, it becomes a deadly foe.'
segments.s750-g271: دگر here means 'the other / furthermore'. آنک is a classical relative 'that which / which', and گفتی functions as the relative verb 'you said' (classical past 2nd person), so the phrase picks out 'the other matter which you spoke of'. ز کار سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'about the work/affairs of the army' with ز = 'about/regarding' and سپاه 'the army'.
segments.s750-g272: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; در بوم‌هاشان is locative 'in their homelands' (بوم‌ها + possessive شان 'their'); نشاندم is 1st-person singular past 'I placed/posted' (transitive perfective). به راه is an adverbial phrase 'on/along the road' or 'on the way' describing where/ how they were placed. Together: 'that which I set in their homelands on the road.'
segments.s750-g233: فراوان is an adverb placed sentence-initially to qualify the action ('frequently'/'much'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/about' introducing the topic (نامش = 'his name', name + enclitic pronoun ش). سخن is the noun 'speech/talk' and راندیم is the 1st-person plural past verb; in classical poetic usage راندن can mean to utter or put forth, so the verb at the end gives the meaning 'we uttered/spoke'. Word order: Adverb + prepositional phrase + object + verb (past).
segments.s750-g234: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'finally/at last'. باد here functions as an optative/exclamatory element ('may/the wind...') rather than the literal noun only, and is followed by آورش (آور + -ش 'him') 'bring him'. خواندیم is a past 1st-person-plural verb 'we called/prayed/uttered (a wish)'; together باد آورش خواندیم expresses 'we uttered the wish that the wind bring him'.
segments.s750-g235: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following time expression. The numeric phrase 'بیست و شش سال تا سی و هشت' is a range ('twenty-six years up to thirty-eight'), with و functioning as the connective 'and' inside the numerals and تا meaning 'up to/to' to indicate the span. The time expression precedes the main clause.
segments.s750-g236: بجز means 'except' and governs the following phrase; به‌آرزو literally 'by/with desire' (meaning 'only in hope' or 'merely by wishing'). چرخ ('wheel') here denotes the wheel of fortune (fate). بر ما is a locative/benefactive phrase 'on/upon us', and نگشت is the past negative form meaning 'did not turn/happen' (here 'the wheel did not turn for us'). Word order: exception phrase + subject (چرخ) + locative + negative past verb.
segments.s750-g237: همه 'all' + مهتران ('mahterān' = great ones/chiefs; here the plural subject) begins the clause. خود is reflexive/emphatic 'their own'. تن 'body' is the object and آسان 'easy' describes it; بدند is the classical 3rd-plural past copular/auxiliary 'they were/they made'—the phrase تن آسان بدند idiomatically means 'they relaxed/took bodily ease'. Word order: subject + emphatic reflexive + object + adjective + verb.
segments.s750-g238: بد اندیش is a compound-like adjectival phrase 'bad-thinking/ill-disposed' (بد = 'bad/evil', اندیش = 'thought' used as 'thinking'). یک سر literally 'one head' functions adverbially here to mean 'each one' or 'one and all'. هراسان is the predicate adjective 'afraid/terrified' and بدند is again the 3rd-plural past 'they were'. Overall: 'being ill‑minded, each one was terrified.'
segments.s750-g239: همان 'that/same' + چون used here as a temporal conjunction 'as/when' introduces a subordinate clause. شنیدم is the 1st‑singular past 'I heard' and ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; فرمان تو 'your command/decree' is the source of the news. Word order: demonstrative + conjunction + verb + prepositional source phrase.
segments.s750-g240: جهان را uses the definite-object/topic marker را to put emphasis on 'the world'. بد آمد is a fixed expression meaning 'it went/turned badly' or 'it caused distress' (literally 'fell bad'); here it conveys that the world was troubled/upset. ز introduces the cause 'because of' and پیمان تو 'your covenant/pledge' is the reason. The verb آمد functions as the past of 'to come/happen' in the idiom بد آمد.
segments.s750-g57: فدا کرده is a compound verbal phrase (fada karda) meaning 'has offered/sacrificed' with کرده as the verbal participle; جان را is the definite direct object marked by را; همی is the classical/historical particle indicating continuous or habitual aspect ('always, continually'); پیش من is a locative/dative phrase 'in my presence / before me'. Word order here is verb + object + adverbial.
segments.s750-g58: به introduces a dative/benefactive phrase 'to/for'; دل هم‌زبان is a compound meaning 'a heart that speaks the same language' i.e. 'a like‑minded heart'; و links the two targets; به تن خوی‌شِ من (written here as separate tokens خوی ش من) means 'to my own body/person' — خویش (= 'one's own') is formed with the clitic ش plus the possessor من. These are recipients/beneficiaries of the preceding action.
segments.s750-g59: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خون پدر is a genitive phrase 'father's blood' (i.e. the blood shed of the father); بود is the past copula 'was/there was'; و links the clauses; درد جگر is an idiomatic compound 'pain of the liver' meaning 'heartache' or deep emotional pain (جگر = seat of feeling).
segments.s750-g60: نکردیم is the past-negative first‑person‑plural 'we did not do/perform' (here 'did not show/manifest'); سستی is the noun 'weakness/slackness' as the object of نکردیم; به introduces the target 'with/regarding' and خون پدر repeats 'our father's blood' — idiomatically 'we did not slacken in avenging (or defending) our father's blood.'
segments.s750-g61: بریدیم is the past first‑person‑plural 'we cut (off)'; بندوی را is the definite object 'the bondman' marked by را; دست و پای lists the body parts 'hand(s) and foot/feet' — here the verb governs the following nouns as the things cut off. Classical Persian often places the verb before its objects.
segments.s750-g62: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; کرد is the past 'did/made/put' (subject omitted); بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/for the king' (i.e. concerning the king or where he was placed); تاریک جای literally 'a dark place' (تاریک modifying جای). Together this asks rhetorically where he placed the king — in a dark place.
segments.s750-g63: چو is the temporal 'when'; گستهم here is the proper name 'Gostaham' (written like the verb form) and is the subject; شد is the past 'became'; در جهان is 'in the world'; ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/vanished from sight' (شد ناپدید = 'became vanished' = 'vanished').
segments.s750-g64: ز (poetic form of از) means 'from'; گیتی is 'the world/earth'; یکی here is indefinite 'a certain' or 'one (of)'; گوشه‌ای is 'a corner / a secluded spot' with the indefinite suffix -ای; برگزید is the past 'chose/selected' — subject omitted but understood to be the same person (he chose a secluded spot in the world).
segments.s750-g281: سواران پراگنده کردم is a past‑tense, first‑person clause: سواران (subject) + پراگنده (adjective 'scattered') + کردم ('I caused/did'). Here کردم (past) functions causatively/actively: 'I made/caused (them) scattered' — in natural English, 'I scattered the horsemen' — and به مرز places the destination after the verb phrase (prepositional phrase 'to/toward the frontier').
segments.s750-g282: پدید آمد اکنون ز ناارز ارز is an independent clause: پدید آمد (past intransitive 'appeared') with اکنون ('now') as temporal adverb. The prepositional phrase ز ناارزِ ارز uses ز = 'from' (poetic), ناارز = negative prefixed adjective 'unworthy/worthless' modifying ارز ('value/worth'), so literally 'appeared now from the unworthy value.' The word order (verb before the adverbial phrase) is typical of classical/epic Persian.
segments.s750-g283: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). از هر سوی = a prepositional phrase 'from every direction/quarter' (از + هر + سوی). بازخوانی سپاه is a deverbal/nominal phrase in which بازخوانی functions as a verbal noun ('calling back / recalling / rallying') modifying سپاه ('the army'); together it means roughly 'the army being summoned/called together.'
segments.s750-g284: گشاده ببیند بد اندیش راه: گشاده ('open') is an adjective placed before the verb ببیند ('sees' — here present/subjunctive form used in the subordinate temporal clause after چو, commonly rendered 'when … sees' or 'if … sees'). بد اندیش is a compound idea 'evil‑minded'/'ill‑intentioned' (bad + thought), functioning as the subject/agent that performs ببیند; راه ('way, path') is the object modified by گشاده. So the literal order is 'the ill‑minded see (an) open way.'
segments.s750-g285: که is the conjunction 'that/for' introducing an explanatory clause. ایران is the subject. چوباغیست is a fused/formal poetic expression equivalent to چوِ باغی است ('is like a garden') — an archaic/epic concatenation where چو + باغی + است appears as one token; خرم بهار is a descriptive noun phrase 'joyful/pleasant spring' (خرم modifying بهار).
segments.s750-g286: شکفته همیشه گل کامگار strings together modifiers and predicates: شکفته ('blossomed' or 'in bloom') applies to گل ('flowers'), همیشه ('always') is an adverb, and کامگار ('fulfilling desire' / 'satisfied') is a qualifying adjective describing the flowers. The clause therefore reads 'flowers ever in bloom and satisfying/wish‑fulfilling.'
segments.s750-g287: پر از + a list names what the place is 'full of' (پر از = 'full of'). The coordination نرگس و نار و سیب و بهی lists flowers/fruits: نرگس (narcissus), نار (here a fruit name in epic diction, listed with سیب and بهی), سیب (apple), بهی (quince/related to quince). The conjunction و links the items; in epic poetry short fruit names may appear (نار for pomegranate/fruit) even if modern usage differs.
segments.s750-g288: چو introduces 'when'. پالیز (paliz) is an old/poetic word for 'orchard / cultivated field'. گردد is the present/subjunctive/passive‑forming verb 'becomes' (گَرد + ـد used as 'become'); ز مردم تهی = 'empty of people' where ز = 'of/from' and تهی = 'empty.' So the temporal clause reads 'when the orchard becomes empty of people.'
segments.s750-g137: The initial که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). هرکس ... که is a correlative/relative pair meaning 'whoever' / 'whosoever'; the second که functions as the relative 'who'. او is the pronoun 'he/that one' (subject). دشمن is the predicate noun 'enemy', and ایزدست is an archaic fused copula meaning 'is (of) God' — together دشمنِ ایزدست = 'is an enemy of God'. Word order is Subject (هرکس/او) + Predicate (دشمن ایزدست) with the copular element incorporated in ایزدست.
segments.s750-g138: ورا is the clitic pronoun 'to him/for him' (marks the person affected). در جهان زندگانی is a locative phrase 'in the world / in this life' with جهان here used as 'this life/existence' and زندگانی 'life'. بدست literally 'in (the) hand(s)' — here idiomatically 'in the hands of others' or 'at the mercy/control (of others)'. The sequence puts the affected person before the locative phrase and the idiomatic predicate.
segments.s750-g139: به زندان 'to/into prison' is a directional phrase; ما 'our' qualifies زندان. ویژه is an adjective 'special/reserved (for)'; the following noun دیوان (classical plural of دیو) here names those for whom the prison is set aside ('for devils/evil ones'). بدند is an old plural verbal form meaning 'they gave/they put/they consigned' (a past or optative sense in epic style). Word order is prepositional phrase + possessive + adjective + beneficiary + verb.
segments.s750-g140: که introduces the purpose/explanatory clause 'so that / such that'. نیکان 'the good (people)' is the agent in the following clause. ازیشان is the compound preposition/phrase 'from/of them' (از + ایشان) marking source/possession. غریوان is a poetic word for 'collars' or 'necklaces' (here 'collars' as a mark of honor/ornament). بدند repeats the same old-form verb 'they gave/they took off' — context shows 'the good took their collars (from them)'.
segments.s750-g141: چو here means 'since/as/when' giving a causal clause. ما را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker with 'we' as the topic. نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'there was not / we did not have' (i.e., 'we lacked'). پیشه 'profession/habit' and خون ریختن 'shedding blood' form a nominal phrase 'a practice of bloodshed' — together: 'we had no practice of shedding blood.'
segments.s750-g142: بدان is the compound 'به + آن' = 'to/for that', here pointing to the previously mentioned affair. کار 'work/affair' is qualified by تنگ 'narrow' (here 'difficult/restricted'). اندر آویختن is an infinitival/nominalized verbal phrase 'to cling to / to take up' — so the whole segment = 'to take up that narrow/difficult task.' The verb is in the infinitive/gerund style, functioning as the complement of the prior clause.
segments.s750-g143: بدان را = 'that one' (ب + دان/آن) with را marking it as the object/topic. به زندان 'into prison' states location. همی is the continuous/habitual particle 'always/continually' found in epic diction. داشتم is the past 1st‑person 'I had / I used to keep' — together: 'I used to keep that one in prison.'
segments.s750-g144: گزند کسان is a genitival phrase 'the harm/insult (of) people' where گزند = 'harm' and کسان = 'people/others'. خوار is an adjective 'despised/humiliated'. نگذاشتم is the negated past 'I did not allow / I did not let' (negative + گذاشتم), so the clause says 'I did not allow the harm of people to make him despised.'
segments.s750-g161: ز کس ما نجستیم جز باژ و ساو is organized as a negative existential-seeking clause: ز + کس marks the source/agent 'from anyone' and نجستیم (past) is the verb 'we sought'. The particle جز introduces the exception 'except', so the whole means 'we sought from no one except...'. Word order is classical Persian (preposition + noun, subject pronoun after the noun, verb after subject).
segments.s750-g162: هر آنکس که او داشت با باژ تاو is a relative clause modifying an implied result or remark: هر آنکس که ('whoever, any person who') + او داشت (he had/possessed) + با (with) + باژ تاو (Bāzh’s radiance). The verb داشت is past tense 'had'; با functions as the preposition 'with' linking the possessor to the quality 'باژ تاو'. The clause therefore reads 'whoever had with him Bāzh’s taw (radiance)'.
segments.s750-g163: ز یزدان پذیرفتم آن تاج و تخت: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from God'. پذیرفتم is first-person past 'I accepted' (پذیرفتن). آن تاج و تخت is the direct object ('that crown and throne'); the coordinating و links تاج and تخت. Word order is prepositional phrase – verb – object, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s750-g164: فراوان کشیدم ازان رنج سخت: کشیدم is the first-person past of کشیدن and here means 'I endured/suffered'. فراوان is an adverb 'much/abundantly' modifying کشیدم. ازان ('from that') introduces the source of suffering; رنج سخت is the noun phrase 'severe suffering'. The clause reads 'I endured much from that severe suffering.'
segments.s750-g165: جهان‌آفرین داور داد و راست strings several titles and a noun: جهان‌آفرین 'world-creator' and داور 'judge' are two epithets; داد here is a noun 'justice' (not a verb) and راست 'truth' or 'right' is coordinated with داد by و. In classical Persian poetry such nominal lists are common: 'the creator, the judge — justice and truth.'
segments.s750-g166: همی روزگاری دگرگونه خواست: خواست is past 'willed/desired'. روزگاری here functions as 'fate/time' (the agent of willing), and دگرگونه 'otherwise/different' is the object/adjunct: 'fate willed otherwise.' همی is a classical particle that often accompanies verbs in narrative poetry (an intensifying/iterative particle) and here helps link the clause to the narrative 'and so/then fate willed differently.'
segments.s750-g167: نیم دژمنش نیز در خواست او: دژمنش is a possessive compound 'his enemy/foe'. نیم here (literally 'half') is used in a distributive or adverbial sense 'even half' or 'some part' and نیز 'also' adds emphasis. در + خواست او can be read as 'desired him' (خواست as 'wanted/sought' with در giving a circumstantial sense). The overall sense: 'even part/half of his enemies also desired him.'
segments.s750-g168: فزونی نجوییم در کاست او: فزونی 'increase' is the noun; نجوییم here is a first‑person‑plural form of جوییدن/جویدن (search/ask) and in context functions as 'we sought/asked for (no) increase' — the construction is elliptical and classical (subject implied). در کاست او literally 'in his diminution/lessening' (کاست as the past participle/nominalized form 'decrease'). Together: 'we did not seek increase in/when his share was diminished.'
segments.s750-g313: سخنها (subject, plural) + نه (negator) + از یادگار تو (prepositional phrase) + بود (past copula). Word order is subject–predicate: literally 'the words were not from your memorial.' Here نه negates the following prepositional predicate. بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s750-g314: که introduces the explanatory/contrasting clause 'that ... was ...'. گفتار آموزگار تو is a possessive phrase (the speech/utterance of your teacher) with گفتار as the noun and آموزگار تو as its possessor; بود is again the past copula ('was').
segments.s750-g315: وفا کردن او is a verbal-nominal phrase: وفا (fidelity) + کردن (light verb 'to do') + او (object: 'him'). The phrase و از ما جفا contrasts with it: از marks the target of جفا (cruelty) and ما is 'us' — so 'being faithful to him and doing injustice toward us.'
segments.s750-g316: تو خود (you yourself) + کی (interrogative 'when') + شناسی (recognition/know) — here شناسی functions as 'will you know/recognize' (second-person present/future sense). The contrast phrase جفا از وفا ('cruelty from fidelity') is the thing to be distinguished: the preposition از means 'from' (i.e. distinguish X from Y).
segments.s750-g317: بدان پاسخش is an archaic locution meaning 'regarding that reply' (بدان = 'to/with respect to that'); it is followed by the vocative ای بد کم خرد ('O bad, little‑minded one'), where بد modifies کم‌خرد (ill / unfortunate + small‑minded). This opens the speaker's remark about the reply.
segments.s750-g318: نگویم is the negative present/future first‑person form 'I will not say' (ن- negation + گویم/گویم → نگویم). جزین نیز = 'except this also' (جز + این). که introduces a subordinate clause; اندر خورد is a verbal phrase meaning 'befell/was applicable' or 'pertained' (اندر = in/within, خورد = befall/apply). So: 'I will say nothing except this, that it pertained.'
segments.s750-g319: تو دعوی کنی is a protasis-like clause 'if/though you make the claim' with کنی as present/subjunctive of کردن used for 'you claim.' هم تو باشی is an emphatic continuation 'even you be' (باشی as optative/subjunctive 'may you be' or 'you even being'), and گوا here means 'witness' (i.e. 'you yourself act as witness'). The sequence expresses allowance or hypothetical concession.
segments.s750-g320: چنین مرد = 'such a man' (demonstrative + noun). بخرد is an adjective 'wise/prudent' placed before ندارد (does not have), and روا ('fitting/permissible') follows as a comment on propriety. The clause means 'it is not fitting/proper to call such a man wise' (literally: 'such a man does not have (the quality) wise — it is not fitting').
segments.s750-g209: بدان سال is a temporal prepositional phrase: بـ (here the prefix/preposition meaning “in/at”) + دان (an archaic/poetic element meaning ‘that’), giving “in that year.” تا is the conjunction “until.” باژ is the noun “cry/noise” and جستم is the past first‑person singular of جستن used here in the sense “I sought/tried.” شمار is the object “count/number” that the verb jostam takes (i.e. “I tried to count”).
segments.s750-g210: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” شد is the simple past third‑person singular of شدن, “became/turned into.” باژ again is the noun “cry,” and دینار is the money unit “dinar.” بر here functions as a preposition meaning “to/amounting to” introducing the quantity صد هزار (“a hundred thousand”). The clause as a whole says that the cry, when converted into dinars, came to 100,000.
segments.s750-g211: پراگنده is an adverb/adjectival predicate meaning “scattered/widely.” افگند is the simple past third‑person singular of افکندن, “he threw/cast.” پنداوسی is a proper name or label (object of افگند). The verb phrase therefore means “he cast Pendausi about/widely.”
segments.s750-g212: همه is the determiner “all/every.” چرم is the noun “leather/skin.” پنداوسی again names the owner or kind (“of Pendausi” in a genitive relation). پارسی is an adjective “Persian/of Persia.” The clause is a nominal phrase with an implied copula (common in classical Persian): “all the leather of Pendausi (was) Persian.”
segments.s750-g213: به is the preposition “to/for.” هر is the distributive “each/every.” بدره‌ای (بدره + ی‑ـ) is a poetic noun, literally “a full‑moon” used idiomatically for a beauty or a beloved; the ـای marks indefiniteness/one of them. در here introduces the amount (classical usage: در + number = “to the amount of”), and ده و دو هزار is a classical numeric phrasing equivalent to 12,000 (literally “ten and two thousand” in older ordering). The whole phrase reads “for each badre, to the amount of twelve thousand.”
segments.s750-g214: پراگنده functions adverbially again: “scattered/widely.” دینار is the object “dinars.” بد here is an archaic/poetic spelling or variant of the prepositional idea “to/for” (the manuscript form varies) linking the distributed sum to its recipient; شاهوار is either an epithet meaning “regal/majestic” or a name/title of the recipient. Together the clause says he scattered dinars—twelve thousand each—(as) royal largesse/for the regal one.
segments.s750-g215: جز از is a standard exception phrase “except from/except for.” باژ و دینار coordinates two nouns “cry and dinar(s).” هندوستان is a post‑nominal genitive/origin “Hindustan/India.” In context the phrase excludes the cries and the dinars coming from (or belonging to) India. Grammatically, هندوستان most directly modifies the nearest noun but by coordination can be read as applying to both.
segments.s750-g216: Again جز از introduces an exception “except from.” کشور is the noun “country/realm.” روم is the place name “Rome” (here meaning Byzantium/the Roman realm). و joins the next term, and جادوستان is a poetic place‑name “land of magic / sorcery.” The clause therefore excludes things coming from the Roman realm and from Jādūstān.
segments.s750-g17: میندیش is an imperative (addressing the listener) meaning 'think/consider'. زین پس is an adverbial phrase 'from now on' (زین + پس); برین سان is a compound 'بر + ین + سان' meaning 'in this manner / this sort of' with the demonstrative element (ین) and سان 'such'. پیام is the noun 'message/warning' and is the object of the imperative.
segments.s750-g18: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / (namely) that'. دشمن شود is a verbal clause where شود (classical/persuasive present/future) means 'become / may be' (literary). بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'against you' and the final بر شادکام is another prepositional phrase functioning adverbially ('in/at (your) prosperity' — an elliptical condition where 'you are' is omitted).
segments.s750-g19: به یزدان is an oath-like prepositional phrase 'by God' (به used as 'by'). مرا کار is an older/poetic possessive/oblique construction where مرا ('my' / 'to me') precedes کار ('affair, business'), and پیراسته‌ست is a perfective/adjectival compound meaning 'is adorned/put in order' with the predicate at the end as in classical Persian.
segments.s750-g20: نهاده is a past participle 'placed / put (down / ordained)'. بران is an adverbial/locative 'upon that / on it' and links to the following noun گیتی‌ام ('my world' — گیتی + possessive suffix -ام). خواسته‌ست is a perfective 'has desired / has willed' and functions here to say that (God) willed or decreed this placement; overall the clause says the placement of the speaker's fate/world was willed.
segments.s750-g21: بدین (here written as ب + دین) functions as 'by this / in this way' introducing the manner of action; جستن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'seeking/searching'. عیبهای دروغ is a noun phrase 'false faults' (عیبهای = plural 'faults'; دروغ qualifying them as fabricated/false). The whole phrase means 'in this search for false faults'.
segments.s750-g22: به نزد بزرگان is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the great ones (elders)'. نگیری is a negative jussive/imperative form (ن + گیری from گرفتن) meaning 'do not take / do not seize / do not remove' addressed to the listener. فروغ is the object 'radiance / splendor' — so the clause cautions against removing or diminishing glory before the elders.
