segments.s750-g23: بیارم is a first-person verb 'I will bring / I bring' (classical/poetic present-future form) serving as the main verb. کنون is an adverb 'now'. پاسخ این همه is the object noun phrase 'an answer to all this' (پاسخ = answer; این همه = all of this) placed after the verb, following classical Persian order.
segments.s750-g24: بدان is an imperative/phrase 'know this' (ب prefix + دان 'know'); تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause 'so that / in order that'. بگویید is the verb 'you (pl./honorific) may say / relate' (here rendered as purpose: 'so that you may tell'), and پیش رمه is a locative 'before/in front of the throng' (پیش = before/in the presence of, رمه = multitude/throng).
segments.s750-g41: گنهکار بهرام is an epithet + name (literally 'guilty/sinful Bahram'); خود is the emphatic/reflexive placed after the name to mean 'himself' (emphatic subject). با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army' that attaches to the verb that follows and indicates accompaniment or means.
segments.s750-g42: بیاراست is a past (poetic) verb 'he/it arrayed, adorned, prepared' with the subject supplied by the preceding phrase. در پیش من is a locative/relative phrase 'before me / in my presence' (در + پیش used idiomatically), and رزمگاه is the direct object 'battlefield/place of combat' receiving the action.
segments.s750-g43: ازو is an archaic/poetic contraction of از او 'from him'; نیز 'also' modifies the clause; بگریختم is 1st person singular past 'I fled/escaped'; روز جنگ is a temporal phrase 'the day of battle' (روز + genitive جنگ) giving the time of the action.
segments.s750-g44: بدان (here a fused particle 'ب + دان' used idiomatically) + تا introduces a purpose/avoidance clause: تا + نیایم is a negative-subjunctive purpose 'so that I do not / lest I should come'. نیایم is the negative present-subjunctive form meaning 'that I not come/that I not be brought'; او را is the definite object with the postposition را, and به چنگ is an idiom 'into the grasp' (چنگ = claw/hold) indicating being seized or caught.
segments.s750-g45: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that/thereupon'; دگر باره باز is a stacked adverbial cluster meaning 'again / once more' (دگر and باره and باز reinforce repetition); آمدم is the 1st person past 'I came/returned' and is the main verb of the clause.
segments.s750-g46: دلاور is a nominative label 'the brave one / hero' (here appositive or predicate identifying the speaker). به جنگ‌ش is a prepositional phrase 'to/into his battle' (جنگ‌ش = جنگ + -ش 'his/its'), and فراز آمدم is a compound verbal expression 'I rose up / I advanced upward/onto' with فراز giving the sense 'on high / upward / into the fray' and آمدم again in 1st person past.
segments.s750-g47: نه negates the following nominal phrase; پرخاش بهرام is a genitive construction 'Bahram's attack/aggression' (پرخاش = assault/aggression), یکباره 'sudden/all at once' qualifies the event, and بود is the past copula 'was' — together 'there was not a sudden assault by Bahram.'
segments.s750-g48: جهانی here means 'the whole world / everyone' (an indefinite collective subject). بر آن جنگ is a locative/target phrase 'upon that battle' (بر + آن + جنگ), نظاره بود is the past progressive/state 'was observing / was a spectacle to behold' (نظاره = observation/view), so the clause says 'the whole world was watching that battle.'
segments.s750-g353: This short clause is a compact description: دگر 'another/additionally' (preposed emphasis), ویژه 'special' qualifies the following noun phrase; با جل دیبه is a prepositional phrase 'with (با) glorious (جل) brocade/robe (دیبه)'. بدند is an archaic past/plural verb-form meaning 'they were' (used here to indicate that they were thus dressed). Word order is adjective(s)+noun then verb, typical of older Persian poetic lines.
segments.s750-g354: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/so that'; در دشت 'in the plain' (در = in + دشت = plain). با باد همره is an instrumental/adverbial phrase: با باد 'with the wind' and همره here functions adverbially 'accompanying/along with' (not a separate actor). The final بدند repeats the archaic past/plural copula/verb 'they were/went', giving the sense 'they proceeded with the wind accompanying them.'
segments.s750-g355: به نزدیک قیصر فرستادم این is unmarked (poetic) word order: به نزدیک قیصر 'to/into the presence of the Caesar' (به + نزدیک used as a locative phrase), فرستادم is first-person past 'I sent', and این 'this' is the direct object placed after the verb for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.s750-g356: پس از خواسته خواندمش آفرین: پس از خواسته is a temporal/causal phrase 'after (or at the) request' (پس = then/after, از = from/of in the temporal sense), خواندمش is 'I read/recited it to him' (خواندم + object enclitic -ش), and آفرین is an interjection 'Bravo' or an exclamation appended to the recital. The clause thus means 'at his request I read it and exclaimed “Afarin.”'
segments.s750-g357: ز دار مسیحا is a compact genitival phrase: ز = 'from/about/concerning' (poetic), دار مسیحا = 'the tree/gallows/cross of the Messiah' (دار here = cross/gallows/tree). که گفتی سخن is a relative/comment clause 'which you spoke of' (که = that/which; گفتی here corresponds to the expression 'you told/you mentioned' and سخن = 'word/speech' complements it). The whole phrase frames the topic 'about the Messiah's cross that you spoke of.'
segments.s750-g358: به گنج اندر افگنده چوبی کهن: به...اندر افگنده is a verbal phrase meaning 'has cast into' (به + اندر giving direction 'into/within' and افگنده = 'thrown/laid/placed', perfect participial form used with the implied subject). چوبی کهن is the object 'an old piece of wood' (چوبی 'wooden/piece of wood', کهن 'old/ancient'). Word order is verb phrase then the describing object, typical in poetry.
segments.s750-g359: نبد زان مرا هیچ سود و زیان is an archaic/poetic negation: نبد is an older form meaning 'there was not' (equivalent to نبود/نبودند), زان 'from that' (indicating source or consequence), مرا (me/to me) + را (object marker) mark the experiencer/indirect object, and هیچ سود و زیان means 'any profit or loss'. Together: 'From that there was no profit or loss to me.' Note that مرا را is split in the word list but functions as the dative/object 'to me'.
segments.s750-g360: ز ترسا شنیدی تو آواز آن is a question/confirmation: ز = 'from' or 'about', ترسا = 'the pious man/monk', شنیدی 'you heard' (second-person past), تو 'you' emphatic subject, آواز آن 'that voice'. Literally: 'Did you hear that voice from the monk?' The clause uses the past-tense شنیدی for a completed action and puts the subject pronoun تو after the verb for emphasis (poetic order).
segments.s750-g97: This is a verbless nominal phrase listing two items: 'یکی' introduces the count/indefinite 'one/a certain' (here numeral/indefinite), followed by noun + adjective pairs. Word order is noun then modifier: 'تیغ هندی' = 'Indian sword', 'پیل سپید' = 'white elephant'. The whole phrase functions as an asyndetic item-list (poetic compression) where the copula is omitted.
segments.s750-g98: 'جزین' (= 'apart from these/except these') opens a contrastive phrase. 'هرچ' is an indefinite 'whatever/anything', and 'بودم' is the 1st-person past copula 'I was' used here in an existential/possessive sense ('what I had/was for me'). 'به گیتی' is a locative/limited phrase 'in the world', and 'امید' is the predicate 'hope' — together: 'apart from these, whatever I had in the world was hope.' Note the older/poetic word order where the predicate follows the locative phrase.
segments.s750-g99: This is another asyndetic inventory: nouns listed in sequence. The first item 'ابا' here functions as an item in the list (a cloak/outer garment in context), followed by 'تیغ' (sword) and the compound 'دیبای زربفت' (dībā-ye zarbaft = brocade woven with gold) — adjectives/qualifiers follow the noun. The line compresses items without a verb (typical in epic inventories).
segments.s750-g100: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; 'هر گونه‌ای' = 'every kind/type'. 'اندرو' = 'in it/among them' (adverbial particle pointing back to the listed items). 'برده رنج' is a light verbal construction: 'برده' (past participle/part of a compound verb 'to have borne/undertaken') + 'رنج' (hardship/toil) yields 'had borne toil' or 'took pains'. Together: 'from every kind of them he had borne toil.'
segments.s750-g101: 'سوی تو' is the prepositional phrase 'to/toward you'. 'یکی نامه بد' is the noun phrase 'a certain bad/unfavorable letter' (adjective follows noun). 'بر پرند' = 'upon/on a bird' specifies the medium (the letter bound to a bird). This fragment is a compressed clause stating the addressee and the item sent.
segments.s750-g102: 'نوشته' here is the participial adjective/noun 'written' (i.e., the writing). 'چو من دیدم' = 'when I saw' (چو used as subordinating conjunction 'when'); subject 'من' + past 'دیدم'. 'از خط هند' is a prepositional phrase 'from/by the Indian hand/script' indicating the origin/style of the handwriting. The clause as a whole: 'when I saw the (hand)writing, (it was) from the Indian script.'
segments.s750-g103: 'بخواندم' is 1st-person past 'I read/recited' (here reporting what he read). It is followed by an appositive nominal clause introduced by 'یکی' = 'a certain' (here indefinite/person introducer) and the head noun 'مرد هندی' = 'an Indian man', with 'دبیر' (secretary/scribe) as an appositive/title. The phrase represents the content of what was read.
segments.s750-g104: This segment lists epithets joined by 'و' ('and'): 'سخن‌گوی' = 'speaker/eloquent', 'داننده' = 'knower/learned', 'یادگیر' = 'learner/memorizer'. Grammatically these are predicate or appositive adjectives that further characterize the previously mentioned man; the serial 'و' links coordinate adjectives.
segments.s750-g145: بسی گفت is a compressed classical phrase meaning 'many have said' or 'it has often been said' (بسی = much/many, گفت = said). هرکس که آن دشمنند is a relative clause 'whoever (هرکس) that (که) is of that (آن) enemy'; note the predicate دشمنند uses a plural/old-fashioned ending -ند (here acting as 'are') even though هرکس is formally singular — a common classical generic usage.
segments.s750-g146: ز تخم بدانند = 'are known/from the seed' where ز = 'from' and تخم = 'seed/lineage'; بدانند (here a passive or intransitive sense) means 'are considered/known' (they are regarded as). و آهرمنند 'and are Ahriman-like/devilish' uses آهرمنند as a predicative plural 'they are Ahriman/devils.' The clause states a general judgment about lineage.
segments.s750-g147: چو اندیشه ایزدی داشتیم = 'when we had a divine thought/intent.' چو here functions as a temporal 'when'; اندیشه = 'thought/intention' and ایزدی qualifies it as 'divine'; داشتیم is the past plural 'we had.'
segments.s750-g148: سخنها همی خوار بگذاشتیم = 'we put aside the speeches/words as despised.' سخنها = 'words/talks' (plural), همی is the continuous/classical particle (here marking habitual/ongoing action), خوار = 'despised/worthless,' and بگذاشتیم is the past 'we put/left (aside).' Together: we dismissed ordinary talk as worthless.
segments.s750-g149: کنون من شنیدم که کردی رها = 'Now I heard that you did (کردی) set free/let go (رها).' کنون = 'now,' من = 'I,' شنیدم = 'I heard/learned (of),' که introduces reported content, کردی is the 2nd-person past 'you did,' and رها is a participle/adjective 'released/freed.'
segments.s750-g150: مر آن را که بد بتر از اژدها = 'to me (مر) that (آن) — (object marker را) — which is bad, worse than a dragon.' مر = 'to me,' را = object marker attached to آن, که introduces the qualifying clause, بد = 'bad/evil,' بتر = comparative 'worse,' از = 'than,' and اژدها = 'dragon.' The clause specifies what was released.
segments.s750-g151: ازین بد گنهکار ایزد شدی = 'From this (از این) bad sinner (بد گنهکار) you became/is become god (ایزد شدی).' ازین is a contracted form of از + این 'from this,' بد گنهکار is a noun phrase 'wicked sinner,' ایزد can be read as predicate or vocative 'god,' and شدی is the 2nd-person past 'you became.' The word order puts the source phrase first and the resulting state (ایزد شدی) after it.
segments.s750-g152: به گفتار و کردارها بد شدی = 'in speech and deeds you became bad.' به here marks the domain ('in/by'), گفتار و کردارها = 'speech and deeds' (کردارها is plural), بد = 'bad/evil,' and شدی is again 'you became.' This clause states the change manifested in both words and actions.
segments.s750-g273: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from' placed before the cause; بی‌دانشی is a genitive/causal noun meaning 'ignorance'. این is the demonstrative referring back to the foregoing action or thing. نیاید is an impersonal/predicate form ('does not come/it is not fitting') used with پسند (a noun) to form the phrase 'نیاید پسند' = 'is not pleasing/fit'. Word order is causal phrase + demonstrative + impersonal predicate + noun (approval).
segments.s750-g274: ندانی is the 2nd-person present (classical/poetic) 'you do not know'. همی is an adverbial particle giving continuity or emphasis ('still/always' or ongoing action). راه is the noun 'way/method' governed by ندانی (you do not know the way). سود از گزند is a small noun phrase 'benefit from harm' with از marking the relation 'from' — the whole clause means 'you cannot distinguish / you do not know the way of benefit from harm.'
segments.s750-g275: چنین است پاسخ is a nominal sentence with چنین 'thus/so' + copula است and the noun پاسخ 'answer' (’Such is the answer’). که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. از رنج من is a prepositional phrase 'from my suffering/hardship' with من as the possessive ('my') placed after the noun as often in poetry.
segments.s750-g276: فراز آمد is a verbal phrase where فراز ('on high, upward') precedes the verb آمد ('came/rose') to give the sense 'rose up' or 'came aloft'. این نامور گنج من is a noun phrase 'this renowned treasure of mine' (این demonstrative + adjective نامور + noun گنج + possessive من). Word order places the adverbial فراز before آمد and the demonstrative+modifier after the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s750-g277: ز بیگانگان is a prepositional/causal phrase 'from/against foreigners'; شهرها is the (direct) object 'the cities' (plural) that follows the cause in this poetic ordering. بستدم is the past tense, 1st-person singular 'I closed/fastened' (بستم with poetic -دم ending). The clause as a whole means 'I closed the cities against strangers.'
segments.s750-g278: همه is the quantifier 'all'; دشمنان is the plural object 'enemies'; را is the definite-object marker marking them as the direct object. به هم is an adverbial phrase meaning 'together' or 'into one' (here indicating a collective or concentrated action). برزدم is past 1st-person 'I struck/assailed/attacked' (I put to flight/struck down). The word order is quantifier + object + object marker + adverbial + verb.
segments.s750-g279: بدان is the imperative formed from ب (the prefix) + دان (to know) meaning 'know this' or 'be aware'. تا is the subordinating conjunction 'so that/in order that' introducing the purpose/result clause. به آرام is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in peace' or 'calmly' (به + آرام). بر تخت ناز is the locative phrase 'upon the throne of nâz' where تخت 'throne' is modified by ناز (here meaning pride/luxury); بر is the preposition 'on/upon'.
segments.s750-g280: نشینیم is a present-subjunctive/optative 1st-person plural 'let us sit' / 'may we sit', used to express desired outcome introduced by تا. بی‌رنج is an adjectival phrase 'without pain/untroubled' (بی- negative prefix + رنج). The following و phrases (و گرم و گداز) list states/qualities: گرم 'warm/ardent' and گداز (from گداز) 'melting/ardor' — together they qualify the desired state while connected to the verb نشینیم.
segments.s750-g169: بجستیم is a past/pluperfect-looking verb form (1st‑pl.) meaning “we sought/asked for”; in classical Persian narrative it stands for a completed action. خشنودی is the object ‘approval/favor’ and دادگر is an epithet ‘the just (ruler/judge)’ placed after the object, so the whole phrase reads literally “we sought the favor of the just one.”
segments.s750-g170: ز بخشش: ز = “by/through” (poetic preposition) and بخشش = ‘gift/grant/charity’; ندیدم is the speaker’s negated perfect/past “I did not see/find.” به کوشش گذر is a verbless nominative phrase functioning as the thing not found: به = “by/through,” کوشش = ‘effort,’ گذر = ‘a way/passage’; together “a way by effort.”
segments.s750-g171: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause “when.” پرسد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive “asks” (classical usage often renders a future sense in subordinate clauses). ز من = “of/from me” (the object of پرسیدن) and کردگار جهان = “the Creator/Lord of the world,” placed after the verb in classical inverted order.
segments.s750-g172: بگویم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive “I will say / I shall tell” used here to indicate the speaker’s intended reply. بدو = ‘to him’ (dative clitic + demonstrative), and آشکار و نهان form a coordinated direct object pair meaning “the manifest (clear) and the hidden.” Word order is verb → indirect object → object(s).
segments.s750-g173: بپرسد here is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive used as a jussive/optative “let him ask” or “if he asks.” که introduces the content clause that follows. او از تو داناترست is a comparative: از marks the standard of comparison (“than”), and داناترست is the comparative adjective “more learned/wiser.”
segments.s750-g174: به هر نیک و بد = “in/with every good and bad (thing)” where به is a prepositional marker and هر = “every.” بر تواناترست is a comparative clause: بر here marks the scope/target of superiority (roughly “over/against”), and تواناترست is the comparative adjective “more able/powerful.” The verb ‘is’ is understood (copula omitted).
segments.s750-g175: همین = “this very” (demonstrative/emphatic) and پر گناهان = “full of sins” (پر = “full (of)”, گناهان = sins). که introduces a relative clause; پیش تواند literally “formerly could/was able” (پیش = before/previously, تواند = 3rd‑person present form used here to state ability in the earlier time reference).
segments.s750-g176: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative “neither ... nor ...” with تیماردار = ‘caretaker/guardian’ and خویش (here written as خوی + ش) = ‘himself/his own.’ The final تواند is the 3rd‑person verb applying to both coordinated subjects: “neither a guardian nor he himself can (do it).”
segments.s750-g257: هم prefaces the noun phrase as an additive/emphatic particle (“also”); آرایش پادشاهی is a noun phrase where پادشاهی qualifies آرایش (poetic prose often omits the ezāfe). بود is the past copula placed at the end, so the whole clause reads literally “also the adornment of kingship was.”
segments.s750-g258: جهان is the subject; بی‌درم (a compound adjective) follows the noun as is typical in Persian poetry to mean “impoverished/without money”; در تباهی is a prepositional phrase “in ruin,” and the clause closes with بود, the past copula (“was in ruin”).
segments.s750-g259: شود is the present/subjunctive form of شد used here as “becomes/should become”; it governs the following nominal description. The sequence بی‌درم شاه بیدادگر is a noun phrase describing the subject (the king) with two adjectival qualifiers (penniless, tyrannical) placed after the noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s750-g260: تهی‌دست is the topicalized noun “the poor/empty‑handed one”; را is the object/topic marker emphasizing that noun; نیست negates existence/possession (“there is not/has not”); هوش و هنر are conjoined nouns “wit/intellect and skill/art.” The clause thus says the poor man lacks both intellect and skill.
segments.s750-g261: به بخشش is a prepositional/qualifying phrase (“in/with regard to giving”); نباشد is a negative copular form meaning “there is not” (poetically placed before the possessed/locative phrase); ورا is a pronominal clitic meaning “to him/for him” and links to دستگاه, which here means “court/establishment.” Word order is inverted for poetic effect: ‘In generosity there was not his court’ → ‘His court had no generosity.’
segments.s750-g262: بزرگان is the subject “the great ones.” فسوسیش is an epithet/nickname being assigned; خوانند is the verb “they call” (here used to give a name/epithet), and شاه is the object “the king.” The clause therefore means “the great ones call the king ‘فسوسیش.’”
segments.s750-g263: ار is the conditional particle “if”; ایدون (poetic/archaic) means roughly “should it come/should it happen”; که is the complementizer “that/which”; از تو به دشمن is a prepositional string “from you to the enemy” specifying the source and recipient; رسد is the verb “reaches / happens (to reach).” The clause thus sets up a conditional: “If it should happen that from you it reaches the enemy…”
segments.s750-g264: همی is an intensifier here (“indeed/then”); بت is the noun “idol”; به دست is a possession/agency phrase literally “into the hand(s) of”; برهمن is the agent/recipient “Brahmin”; رسد is the verb “comes/arrives.” Together: “then the idol will indeed come into a Brahmin’s hand.”
segments.s750-g217: جز از is a fixed poetic phrase meaning “except for” or “apart from”; it introduces the exception list that follows. The nouns بعدِ آن (باژ, وز, ساو) are a string of items/terms grouped together before the final phrase هر کشوری, which specifies the scope (“from every country”). وز here functions as an archaic/poetic connective between nouns rather than the common modern conjunction و, so word order lists items in sequence.
segments.s750-g218: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” هر نامداری و هر مهتری is a parallel coordinate phrase: هر modifies both نامداری and مهتری, and و simply links the two nouns. The sequence clarifies that the exception applies “from every notable (renowned person) and every chief/elder.”
segments.s750-g219: جز از again introduces an exception. رسم و آیین are near‑synonymous nouns joined by و (“and”) to emphasize ceremony/ritual; نوروز و مهر are two named festivals/rituals appended after them. مهر here refers to the deity/festival (an older, proper-name sense) rather than the abstract ‘affection.’
segments.s750-g220: از marks the source/part of the exception (“from”/“for”). اسپان is a plural collective for horses (asp → esp/aspān) and is the object of از. وز again appears as a poetic connective linking the following noun phrase بنده خوب‌چهر, which is a compound: بنده (servant/slave) qualified by the adjective‑name خوب‌چهر (“fair‑faced”).
segments.s750-g221: جز از introduces another list of items; جوشن و خود و گوپال و تیغ are four nouns joined by و, forming a coordinated list (armour, helmet/self, a named item or person, and sword). In epic poetry such trios/quadruples list equipment or persons; each noun is simply juxtaposed and linked by و.
segments.s750-g222: ز ما = “from us” or “on our part.” این نبودی is a past negative copula: نبودی is the past form meaning “was” (here negated = “was not”); سینتاکس places the verb after the demonstrative این (“this/these”), so این نبودی = “this/these were not.” کسی را دریغ uses را as the definite object/marker and دریغ as the noun meaning “reluctance/withholding,” so the whole clause means “we did not withhold these things from anyone.”
segments.s750-g223: جز از opens the exception and introduces a series of valuable commodities: مشک (musk), کافور (camphor), خز (fur/velvet), سمور (sable). The nouns are simply coordinated by و and form a list of luxury goods; word order is noun-by-noun in the customary epic enumerative style.
segments.s750-g224: سیاه و سپید are paired adjectives (“black and white”) describing the previous item(s) (the sable/fur). و ز is a continuation (“and from/and for”), and کیمال بور is a noun + epithet: کیمال here takes the proper‑name sense and بور is the epithet “fair‑haired/pale,” so this final phrase names a person (or named thing) qualified by بور. The construction continues the enumerative pattern.
segments.s750-g457: که introduces a subordinate clause here meaning 'for' / 'that' (introducing the reason or condition). چون functions temporally as 'when' (archaic/poetic usage). بخت is the subject 'fortune' or 'fate', modified by the adjective بیدار 'awake' placed after it. گیرد is the 3rd-person singular present of گرفتن ('takes'), and نشیب, a noun 'descent/decline', follows the verb — a common poetic word order where the verb precedes or surrounds its nominal complement.
segments.s750-g458: ز هر گونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'of/from any kind' that qualifies the preceding notion of 'nashīb' (a decline of any sort). دید here is the verbal root of دیدن used after the modal-like word باید; word order is inverted for emphasis (literally 'seen must a rebuke' → بایَد نهیب دید → 'a rebuke must be seen/issued'). باید is the necessity modal 'must/ought', and نهیب is the noun 'rebuke' or 'stern cry' — together they mean 'a rebuke must be given/issued'.
segments.s750-g459: چو is temporal 'when'. روز بهی is a nominal phrase 'a good/beautiful day' (بهی = excellent). بر کسی بگذرد uses the impersonal/experiential construction 'to pass over someone' (بگذرد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of گذرَدن 'to pass'), meaning 'a good day befalls/passes for someone.' Word order keeps the temporal clause initial.
segments.s750-g460: اگر 'if' begins the conditional. باز here means 'again'. خواند is the 3rd-person singular present of خواندن in the sense 'to call/summon' (i.e. 'to call it back'). ندارد is the negative present 'does not have'; خرد is the noun 'wisdom'. The clause literally reads 'if he calls it back again, he does not have wisdom' — an admonition that trying to summon a past good fortune shows lack of understanding.
segments.s750-g461: پیام من is the noun phrase 'my message' (پیام + possessive من). اینست is the copula 'this is' (stressed demonstrative-copula). سوی جهان is a directional/recipient phrase 'to the world' or 'toward the world' placed after the copula, giving the addressee/aim of the message.
segments.s750-g462: The repeated pattern به نزد X (literally 'to/at the presence of X') lists specific addressees. کهان and مهان are archaic/poetic nouns naming important people — here 'the ancients/elders' and 'the great/nobles' (the glosses provided are limited, but the construction marks them as recipients). و simply coordinates the two recipients.
segments.s750-g463: شما نیز 'you as well' is the addressee and نیز adds 'also/likewise.' پدرود is an archaic noun meaning greeting/salutation; باشید is 2nd-person plural present subjunctive/optative used formulaically as a blessing 'may you be.' و شاد links the second wished state 'and be happy.' This is a conventional benediction.
segments.s750-g464: ز من نیز is a prepositional phrase 'concerning me as well' or 'from me also.' بر بد combines the preposition بر + adjective بد meaning 'against (me) in a bad way' or 'ill toward me.' مگیرید is 2nd-person plural present 'you take/hold'; یاد is the noun 'remembrance/memory' and is the object. Literally 'regarding me also, you take/hold an ill remembrance (against me)'; idiomatically this is an admonition asking them not to bear ill feelings — the negative sense ('do not hold') is interpretive and arises from the conventional blessing/request context.
segments.s750-g89: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (introduces a temporal clause). شاهیم is the 1st‑person plural predicative 'our being king / our kingship' (شاه + -یم); شد is the past tense 'became' (شد) that applies to the following temporal measure; سال بر سی و شش is the complement meaning 'the year reached thirty‑six' (literal: 'the year became on thirty‑six'). Word order is poetic: the verb شد governs the temporal phrase سال بر سی و شش.
segments.s750-g90: میان here means 'among / in the midst of'. چنان is the demonstrative/adverbial 'such'. روزگاران is the plural noun 'times, ages' and خوش is the adjective 'pleasant / happy' describing those times; together they form the locative/adverbial phrase 'in such happy days'.
segments.s750-g91: تو داری is the present second‑person construction 'you have / you hold'; combined with به یاد ('in memory of') it functions as 'you remember'. این سخن is the direct object 'this saying / these words'. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the whole clause and placed sentence‑finally (poetic position).
segments.s750-g92: اگر چند is a concessive phrase 'even if / although some (a little)'. بگذشت is the past tense 'passed' (from گذشتن) and governs the oblique phrase بر ما زمان literally 'time passed over us' (= 'time has passed for us'). Note the poetic word order with verb before the topical oblique (بر ما زمان).
segments.s750-g93: مرا is the dative/accusative pronoun 'to me' (here marking the experiencer). نامه آمد is an existential construction 'a letter came' / 'a letter arrived'. ز هندوستان is the source phrase 'from India'. Word order places the recipient (مرا) before the verb for emphasis, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s750-g94: بدم here is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'I was' (equivalent to بودم) and introduces the speaker's state. من is the emphatic subject 'I'. بدان is the compound of ب + آن ('with respect to that / about that'). نیز 'also' adds the speaker to previously mentioned agreement, and همداستان is the predicate adjective 'in agreement / like‑minded'. The clause thus means 'I too was in agreement about that.'
segments.s750-g95: ز رای برین is 'from / by that counsel' (ز = 'from', رای = 'counsel/plan', برین is a poetic/derived form marking 'that / above'). نزد ما means 'with us / in our presence', and نامه بود is the existential 'there was a letter'. The construction z + noun + نزد + ما + نامه بود gives the source and location of the letter.
segments.s750-g96: This is a simple listing of what accompanied the letter. گهر بود is 'there was a gem/jewel'; و is the coordinating 'and'; هر گونه‌ای جامه بود literally 'garments of every kind there were' (هر = every, گونه‌ای = of a kind/sort, جامه = garment). The repeated بود is the existential 'there was'.
segments.s750-g9: بدان گاه is an archaic/contractional temporal phrase (literally be + an → ‘in/at that’). چِندان modifies the following negated verb نداری and marks the extent (‘to such an extent’). خرد is the thing lacking (object), and the subject (تو) is omitted as usual in Persian.
segments.s750-g10: که introduces a result/clause (‘so that’). مغزت is the subject with the enclitic -ت. به heads the instrumental phrase (’by/with’), دانش names the means (‘knowledge’), خرد is the object (‘wisdom’), and پرورد is an older/past-form of پرورـدن (‘to nurture’) placed at the end as the main verb.
segments.s750-g11: به گفتار here means ‘by/with speech’ (به used instrumentally). بی‌بر is a poetic adjective meaning ‘fruitless/without result’. چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause (‘when/if’), and کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive (‘you do/you put’), used here for the ‘if/when you …’ sense.
segments.s750-g12: روان و خرد را is the definite object phrase (را marks the direct object): ‘the soul (spirit) and wisdom’. پر آهو کنی is a compact poetic predicate: پر (here evoking wings/flight) + آهو (gazelle) with کنی (‘you make’), i.e. ‘you make (them) take flight/flake like a gazelle’ — a figurative way to say you make them tremble or flee.
segments.s750-g13: کسی کو is an older-style relative/indefinite ‘someone/whoever’ (کو here functions as a relative particle in classical usage). گنهکار is the predicate used as a label ‘a sinner’, خواند is the past tense verb ‘called’ (here ‘called you X’), and تو را is the definite object with the object marker را.
segments.s750-g14: از آن پس is a temporal phrase ‘from that time on/after that’. جهاندار is the title/label ‘sovereign’ or ‘world‑ruler’. خواند is again the past tense ‘called’, and تو را is the object; the clause states the change of how that someone addresses you after that time.
segments.s750-g15: نباید is an impersonal modal (‘it must not / should not be’). که introduces the subordinate clause. یابد is a present/subjunctive form ‘finds/gets’ used in this dependent construction. بر تو نشست literally ‘a sitting upon you’ (بر = ‘against/on’ + نشست = ‘sitting/seat’), i.e. ‘take a seat against you’ or ‘set themselves up opposed to you’.
segments.s750-g16: بگیرد is the verb ‘seize/take’ (here ‘obtain’). کم و بیش is the fixed idiom ‘little and much’ → ‘more or less / whether small or large’. چیزی is ‘something’, and به دست is the instrumental/result phrase ‘into hand’ (به + دست) meaning ‘in one’s possession’. Together they mean ‘obtain something, whether little or much.’
segments.s750-g25: پس از is a compound prepositional phrase meaning “after” (پس here = after + از). مرگ من is a genitive phrase “my death” (مرگ + pronoun من). یادگاری بود is the past copular clause: یادگاری (memento) is the predicate noun and بود is the past tense copula “was/there remained.”
segments.s750-g26: سخن گفتن is a verbal-noun construction meaning “speaking” or “the saying of words” and functions as the subject. راست modifies یاری (adjective before noun in poetic word order) giving “true/real help.” بود is the past copula ‘was.’ The whole line: “Speaking was a true help.”
segments.s750-g27: چو here means “when/if” introducing a conditional. پیدا کنم is a verbal phrase in which پیدا (found/appearing) combines with کنم (subjunctive/present) to mean “I cause/bring about” or “I bring (it) about.” بر تو marks the target “upon you,” and انبوه رنج is a noun phrase “a multitude/abundance of suffering.” Word order (verb after modifiers) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s750-g28: بدانی is an exhortative/subjunctive form meaning “know (that) / be aware” addressed to the second person. که introduces the content clause. از رنج ما is a prepositional phrase “out of/from our suffering,” خاست is the past verb “arose/was born,” and گنج is the subject “treasure.” The clause follows a classical order with the oblique phrase before the verb and the subject at the end.
segments.s750-g29: نخستین is an adverb/adjectival adverb “first.” که here links or introduces what follows. گفتی is a second-person past/perfect form “you said/spoke” (classical diction). ز هرمز سخن is a prepositional phrase “a word/about Hormoz” with ز meaning “about/of” and سخن “speech/words.”
segments.s750-g30: به بیهوده is an adverbial phrase “in vain / pointlessly” where به is an adverbial preposition and بیهوده is the adverb. از آرزوی کهن gives the source or motive “from an ancient wish/longing” (از = from, آرزوی = desire, کهن = ancient). Together this modifies the previous clause (‘you spoke … in vain because of an old desire’).
segments.s750-g31: ز here means “because of / from.” گفتار بدگوی ما is a noun phrase “the ill-speaking / slanderous speech concerning us” (بدگوی modifies گفتار with ما as its target). را is the classical definite/object marker placed in this older order; پدر is the (definite) subject “the father.” The phrase as a whole sets the cause: “Because of the slanderous words about us, the father ….”
segments.s750-g32: برآشفت is a past verb meaning “(he) became angry / was enraged.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” شد is the past copula “became,” and کار زیر و زبر is an idiomatic predicate “matters/work were turned upside down” (زیر و زبر = under-and-over, i.e. topsy-turvy). The clause describes the resulting disorder after the father’s anger.
segments.s750-g249: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بودی is past tense (2nd-person singular) 'you were' placed before the noun phrase (poetic word order). خردمند نزدیک تو is the noun phrase 'a wise person near you' (نزدیک modifies خردمند and تو marks the locus); overall: 'When a wise man was near you' (the verb precedes the descriptive phrase in classical order).
segments.s750-g250: که introduces the result/explanatory clause 'that/so that'. روشن شدی is a compound meaning 'you became illuminated' (شدی = past of شدن 'to become', 2nd-person). جان تاریک تو is the object 'your dark soul' (جان with the adjective تاریک and the clitic تو). The verb روشن شدی comes before its object in this poetic ordering and yields 'that your dark soul became bright/enlightened.'
segments.s750-g251: به دادن is a gerundial phrase 'by giving' (به used adverbially). نبودی is past negative 'were not' (here functioning as 'there was not / you did not cause'); کسی را زیان is the accusative construction 'loss/harm to anyone' with را marking the definite object. Word order places the adverbial gerund first, then the negative verb and the object.
segments.s750-g252: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/because'. گنجی رسیدی is past 'you reached/attained a treasure' (رسیدی = 2nd-person past of رسیدن used as 'attain/receive'). به ارزانیان attaches the preposition به to a plural agent 'ارزانیان' (here 'the givers/benefactors'), giving the sense 'to/among the givers'—so the clause explains the source or company in which the treasure was gained.
segments.s750-g253: ایا opens a rhetorical/questioning clause ('is it that' / 'perhaps'). پور 'son/descendant' is modified by the adjectives کم روز ('of few days', i.e. short-lived/young) and اندک خرد ('of little wisdom'). The adjectives come after the noun in this construction, a typical classical order meaning 'a son who is short-lived and of small intelligence.'
segments.s750-g254: روانت is روان 'soul/spirit' with the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت 'your'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and links اندیشه ('thought') as the cause. رامش برد is a verbal phrase 'took away tranquility' (برد here functions as past 'took' with رامش as its object). The verb appears at the end, the expected classical placement, yielding 'thought has taken the peace from your soul.'
segments.s750-g255: چنان دان is an imperative construction: چنان 'thus' + دان (imperative/infinitive 'know/consider') meaning 'consider it thus' or 'so know'. که begins the explanatory clause. این گنج من is the noun phrase 'this treasure of mine' (این + گنج + من). پشت تست is a predicative phrase where پشت 'support/back' is linked to the second-person possessive/test (here functioning as 'is yours' in older poetic usage); the phrase means 'is your support.'
segments.s750-g256: زمانه کنون is 'the world/fortune now' (زمانه 'the age/the world' + کنون 'now'). پاک here functions adverbially 'purely/entirely' or 'altogether'. در مشت تست is a locative/metaphorical phrase 'in (your) fist/grasp' (در = 'in', مشت = 'fist/handful', تست = possessive/verb form indicating 'is yours' in the poetic idiom). The sequence states that the world/fortune is now entirely in your hand.
segments.s750-g185: ولیکن is a contrastic conjunction 'but/yet' at the start. من is the 1st‑person subject 'I'. از بهر خود is an older/poetic phrase meaning 'for my own sake' (از + بهر = 'for; for the sake of', with خود as the possessive/emphatic 'own'). کامه is the noun 'desire' and را is the Persian definite/object marker attaching to کامه, so the whole chunk marks 'my desire' as the thing motivating the speaker.
segments.s750-g186: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / so that / who' specifying the wished action. برخواند is the classical third‑person verbal form meaning 'to recite/read aloud' (here jussive/subjunctive sense: 'that he read aloud'). آن پهلوی نامه is the direct object 'that Pahlavi book' (پهلوی = Pahlavi, نامه = book/letter) and را again is the object marker following نامه.
segments.s750-g187: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to the thing just mentioned. در جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase placed between subject and predicate in the poetic word order. یادگاری is the predicate noun 'memento/keepsake' and بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause says 'that was a memento in the world.'
segments.s750-g188: خردمند را places 'the wise (man)' before the verb with را marking him as the affected/experiencer (older Persian can use را for a beneficiary). غمگساری is the predicate, meaning 'consolation/condolence/comfort', and بود is the copula 'was'—together 'it was a consolation to the wise.' The order is topical/poetic: beneficiary before predicate.
segments.s750-g189: پس از ما is a temporal phrase 'after us' (پس = 'after/then' with از adding the 'after' sense here). هر آنکس که is the indefinite relative 'whoever / any person who'. گفتار ما is 'our speech/words' (گفتار = utterance, ما = our), and this whole segment sets up the conditional/relative subject for the next verbs.
segments.s750-g190: بخوانند is a classical third‑person plural form of خواندن ('to read/recite') used here in a general/conditional sense 'if/when they read'. دانند is the corresponding 'they know' ( دانستن ). بازار ما (literally 'our market/bazaar') is the object of دانند. The pair of verbs expresses: 'whoever reads our words will know our market.'
segments.s750-g191: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The next two items name origins: برطاس and چین; in classical/poetic lists وز is a fused form (و + ز) functioning as 'and from' before چین. سپه is the direct object 'army/host' and راندیم is the past plural 'we drove/led/put in motion.' The hairpin word order (objects and place‑names before the verb) is typical of Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s750-g192: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high military commander). به هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'to/in every place' (به = 'to/into', هر جای = 'every place'). بنشاندیم is a causative/past plural 'we set/installed/placed' (we appointed and established him in those places). The clause thus records installing spahbeds everywhere.
segments.s750-g297: کزان پس بود is a temporal frame: کزان 'from that/therefrom' + پس 'then', followed by بود (past copula) to introduce an existential/predicative clause. غارت و تاختن are verbal-noun style nouns ('plunder' and 'charging') coordinated by و and serve as the predicate of بود ('there was plunder and charge').
segments.s750-g298: خروش سواران is a genitive/possessive idea rendered without an explicit ezâfe in poetry: 'the clamor/uproar of the horsemen'. The second coordinated phrase و کین آختن parallels the first: کین 'vengeance' plus the infinitival/verbal-noun آختن — here used poetically as 'taking up/raising' vengeance. (Note: آختن is archaic/poetic; the supplied sense list is imperfect for this usage.)
segments.s750-g299: A simple coordinated list: زن و کودک (women and children) followed by بوم ایرانیان, where بوم + ایرانیان is a genitive construction 'the homeland of the Iranians' (the possessor follows the possessed in this poetic word order). The three items form the objects/targets of the following injunction.
segments.s750-g300: به ... در میان is a prepositional frame: به + اندیشه بد = 'into/onto a bad thought/plan'; در میان 'in the midst/among'. منه here is an archaic negative imperative (مَنه = 'do not [do X]'), so the whole phrase is a prohibition: 'do not set/place (them) into an evil plan among [people]'. This negative imperative sense of منه is not represented in the provided senses list, hence indexed as -1.
segments.s750-g301: چو is the temporal/subordinating conjunction 'when/if'. سالی چنین = 'a year such as this' (adjectival inversion common in poetry). بر تو بر بگذرد uses the preposition بر twice (the first marks direction/over, the second is the prefixed particle used with گذشتن) plus بگذرد (present/subjunctive form of گذشتن) to mean 'passes over you' or 'goes by you'.
segments.s750-g302: خردمند is the subject 'the wise (man/people)'. خواند here means 'will call'/'calls' (to name/call someone something) — a common poetic use where the tense is reported as present/future. تو را is the object with the definite/accusative marker را; بی‌خرد is the predicate complement ('foolish') telling what they will call you.
segments.s750-g303: من ایدون شنیدم is a reporting clause 'I have heard up to now' (ایدون functions adverbially 'until now' or 'thus far' in classical usage; the provided lemma senses do not capture this adverbial meaning). شنیدم is simple past 'I heard/learned'. کجا here introduces the reported clause (roughly 'when/that') and تو مهی is a brief predicate 'you, O splendid/majestic one'.
segments.s750-g304: همه مردم 'all the people' is the object group; ناسزا را is the direct object marked by را ('insults/abuse'). دهی is the present/subjunctive form of دادن used in the subordinate clause after شنیدم and here means 'you give' or 'you inflict' (i.e. 'you insult the people'). Word order is verb-final in the subordinate clause, with the object ra preceding the verb.
segments.s750-g361: شگفت آمدم is an idiomatic past construction: شگفت (an emotional noun/adverb) + آمدم (1st‑person past) = “I was astonished.” زانک is a causal conjunction “because/since,” and چون introduces a simile “like/as.” قیصری follows چون as the comparee: “like a Caesar” (literally ‘Caesar‑like’). Word order: emotion → speaker verb → causal clause → simile.
segments.s750-g362: سر افراز is a compound adjectival phrase: سر (top/head) + افراز (that which raises) → ‘head‑raised’ = “proud/lofty‑headed.” مردی is a noun meaning “a man” used here as a predicate noun; و connects the coordinated predicate نام‑آوری (“renown, fame”). Note the adjective‑like سر افراز qualifies مردی.
segments.s750-g363: همه at the clause head is a universal quantifier “everyone/all.” گرد بر گرد او is a repetitive, emphatic circumlocution meaning “around him” (گرد … گرد = about/around); بر here functions as a directional/prepositional element (‘toward/at’) and the second گرد gives the sense ‘about/around.’ بخردان is the plural of بخرد (wise ones) — a noun phrase placed after the prepositional phrase as the group denoted.
segments.s750-g364: همش here is a contracted colloquial/formalized form meaning ‘all of them’ (همه‌شان). فیلسوفان and موبدان are coordinated plural nouns (“philosophers and mobeds”); the second هم before موبدان functions as an additive particle “also/too.” The conjunction و simply links the two groups.
segments.s750-g365: که introduces a subordinate/exclamatory clause here. یزدان is the noun ‘God’ as the agent; چرا is the interrogative adverb “why.” خواند is the past‑form verb used rhetorically as “would call” / “call (so)” (here sense = ‘to call/summon’). آن کشته را is the definite object (with را) ‘that slain one.’ The clause reads as a rhetorical question: “why would God call that one ‘slain’?”
segments.s750-g366: This string lists descriptive epithets of the object just mentioned. گرین here appears to be a condensed form (likely گر این = ‘if this’) or an irregular/archaic form; none of the supplied senses fits straightforwardly, so its precise sense is unclear in isolation. خشک چوب is a noun phrase ‘dry wood’ (خشک = “dry” qualifying چوب = “wood/stick”). وتبه گشته is a participial description: وتبه (woe/accursed) + گشته (perfect participle ‘become/turned’) → “turned to woe/accursed.” را is the object marker referring back to آن کشته.
segments.s750-g367: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” آن دار بیکار is a noun phrase ‘that tree/trunk (دار) that is useless/futile (بیکار)’ — note that دار can mean ‘tree’ or ‘gallows’; the surrounding lexical choices (خشک چوب earlier) favour the ‘tree/trunk’ sense. یزدان is the subject ‘God’ and بدی is the noun ‘evil’ — the clause asserts a conditional linking the useless tree to divine agency or responsibility for evil (elliptical copula/verb implied).
segments.s750-g368: سر مایه is a compound meaning ‘chief source’ or ‘principal cause’ (سر + مایه → ‘head/source’); اورمزد is the proper name Ahura Mazda (here in apposition as the divine name), and آن بدی (‘that evil’) is the thing whose source is being identified. The clause thus reads: ‘the chief source of that evil is Ahura Mazda,’ with the copula understood.
segments.s750-g329: بدان is an imperative formed from ب + دان (literary 'know'), introducing the clause 'Know (this):'. رزم here means 'battle' and functions adverbially ('in that battle'). یزدان is the subject ('God/divine power'), and مرا را (written as م + را) marks the speaker as the one to whom the relationship applies — 'my(=to me)'. یار بود is the predicate 'was (a) friend/ally' with بود as a past copula.
segments.s750-g330: سپاه جهان is a nominal phrase 'the host/army of the world'. نزد من is a prepositional/relational phrase 'in my presence / before me' (نزد = 'in the presence of'), and خوار بود is the predicate 'was despised/worthless' with بود as past copula.
segments.s750-g331: شنیدند is a past-tense verb 'they heard/learned' and is fronted here for poetic word order. ایرانیان is the agent 'the Iranians'. آنچ بود is a subordinate clause 'what it was' (آنچ = 'that which'), with بود as the copula.
segments.s750-g332: تو را نیز places emphasis on 'you too' (تو + را as object/topic marker). زیشان means 'from them' (ز = از + ایشان), and بباید شنود is a classical modal construction: بباید 'ought/must' + شنود (subjunctive/infinitival 'hear') = 'must hear'. Word order places the auxiliary before the verb in an older register.
segments.s750-g333: مرا نیز چیزی places 'مرا' (me) with را as an object/topic marker and نیز 'also' — 'there is also something for me' or 'I too have something'. که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. بایست is the modal 'must/ought', and کرد is the lexical verb 'to do' forming 'that must be done' (classical construction where بایست + past stem conveys necessity).
segments.s750-g334: به جای is an idiom 'in place of / instead of'. نیاطوس is a proper name (the person in whose stead), and روز نبرد is the temporal phrase 'the day of battle' (روز = day, نبرد = battle). The whole phrase modifies the 'something' that must be done.
segments.s750-g335: ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from/because of'. خوبی 'goodness' and از مردمی 'from the people' are two sources/cause phrases joined by و. کرده‌ام is perfect 'I have done/acted' (present perfect), so the clause says the speaker has acted because of these causes.
segments.s750-g336: به پاداش او = 'as a reward for him' (به = 'for/as', پاداش = 'reward', او = 'him'). روز بشمرده‌ام uses بشمرده‌ام (present perfect 'I have counted/reckoned') — 'I have reckoned the day as his reward.' The object (روز) follows the prepositional phrase, typical of poetic order.
segments.s750-g153: چو + past verb (شدی) forms a temporal clause 'when you became'. مهتر (lit. 'elder/chief') is the predicate of that clause. کار هشیار کن is the main imperative: کار (affairs) is the noun + هشیار (adjective 'alert/prudent') is combined with the imperative کن ('do'/'be') — Persian often uses adjective + کن to mean 'be ADJ' or 'act ADJ', so the clause means 'handle your affairs prudently.'
segments.s750-g154: ندانی is the negative present (2nd person) 'you do not know' (used here concessively/conditional: 'if you do not know'). تو is emphatic 'you'. داننده را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the knower' as the direct object/topic. یار کن is an imperative 'make (him) a companion' or 'befriend (him)' with کن the imperative verb placed after the object phrase (typical Persian word order: object before verb).
segments.s750-g155: مبخشای is a negative imperative/command 'do not forgive' directed at the addressee. بر هر که is a prepositional phrase 'toward/against anyone who…' where هر که introduces the relative 'who/that'. رنجست زوی indicates the offender ('rancḡ/grievance' from him) — رنجست here conveys 'grievance/harm' and زوی ('from him') attaches to it.
segments.s750-g156: اگر چند is a concessive 'even if/though (there be) some…' with چند = 'some/a few'. امید گنجست زوی is a verbless/clipped clause 'hope (of) treasure exists from him' — گنجست (literally 'is treasure' / 'contains treasure') here indicates the prospect of wealth; زوی 'from him'. The whole segment qualifies the previous prohibition: do not forgive even for the hope of gain.
segments.s750-g157: بر آنکس introduces the target 'against that person'. کزو is a fused relative 'from whom' (ک + ز + او). در جهان 'in the world' limits the scope (i.e. 'in the whole world'); جز گزند is an exception phrase 'except harm' — together: 'from whom, in the world, there is nothing except harm.'
segments.s750-g158: نبینی is a negative present (used for future/expected result) 'you will not see'. مر is the dative enclitic 'to me/for me'. او را is the definite object 'him' marked by را. چه کمتر ز بند is an exclamatory/comparative phrase 'anything less than fetters' (چه here an exclamative 'what/how' and ز بند 'than a bond/chain'); the clause means 'you will see for me nothing less than fetters' (i.e. he will bring me nothing but chains).
segments.s750-g159: دگر introduces 'another' or 'else'. آنک is a relative 'that one who'. از خواسته here means 'about/of his desire' (از = concerning, خواسته = what is wished or desired). گفته‌ای in context functions as 'you have said/mentioned' (2nd-person perfect), i.e. 'the one you spoke of'; the exact tense is a classical perfect form referencing the addressee's prior mention.
segments.s750-g160: خردمندی 'wisdom' (a noun) coordinated with رای 'counsel/opinion' by و. بنهفته‌ای is a participial/adjectival predicate 'is hidden/has hidden' (bən- prefixed to هفتِه, classical formation), so the noun phrase states that wisdom and counsel are concealed/latent in that person (copula is implied).
segments.s750-g289: سپرغم is a compound noun (سپر + غم) placed as the direct object; یکایک is an adverb ‘one by one / each’ that modifies the verb; ز بن is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = from) indicating origin (‘from the root’). برکنند is a 3rd-person plural verbal form (classical Persian uses this subjunctive/present form for narrative/habitual actions) meaning ‘they remove/uproot’ and takes the preceding noun phrase and prepositional phrase as its object/adjunct.
segments.s750-g290: همه is a quantifier 'all/every' modifying شاخ; the phrase شاخ نار و بهی lists the kinds of branches (here ‘branches of pomegranate and of the behi/quince’). بشکنند is again a 3rd-person plural verbal form (parallel to برکنند) meaning ‘they break’ — the verb governs the whole NP 'همه شاخ نار و بهی'.
segments.s750-g291: The clause uses an inverted classical copular construction: سپاه و سلیحست = 'is (an) army and arms' where سلیحست represents سِلاح است (fused 'is armed/armament'). دیوار اوی ('his/its wall') is the topic possessed by اوی (the possessive). The verbless/inverted order is typical in Persian poetry, so read as 'دیوار اوی [= subject/topic], سپاه و سِلاح است [= predicate]'.
segments.s750-g292: به introduces a purpose/role 'for/as' (here 'for its fence'); پرچینش is 'its hedge' (پرچین + possessive -ش). بر نیزه‌ها is a locative phrase 'on/against the spears'. خار اوی ('its thorn(s)') is the noun phrase whose role is predicative/constitutive of that fence. The clause order (به X بر Y Z اوی) is poetic: 'for its hedge, his thorns (خار اوی) were on spears.'
segments.s750-g293: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بفگنی is an archaic/uncertain 2nd-person verbal form (classical subjunctive/optative shape, here 'if you ...'); خیره is an adverb/adjective 'astonished/with mouth agape' modifying the verb; دیوار باغ is the object/topic 'the garden wall'. The clause reads 'If you are struck/astonished at the garden's wall...'.
segments.s750-g294: The repeated چه ... و چه ... construction is correlative, meaning 'whether ... or ...'; each چه has the sense 'whatever / whether'. The sequence enumerates places (باغ, دشت, دریا, راغ) linked by و ('and'), giving a list of possible settings — 'whether garden, plain, sea, or rāgh.'
segments.s750-g295: نگر is an imperative 'look/see/attend!'. تا functions here as a subordinating conjunction with a prohibitive/purpose sense 'so that / lest / that (you do not...)'. تو is the 2nd-person subject pronoun. دیوار او is 'his wall' (او = he/it). نفگنی in this line is a classical/poetic verbal/adjectival form with a negative prohibitive sense in context ('do not become / do not behave in a certain way'); here it functions as the verbal element of the subordinate clause introduced by تا (i.e. 'so that you do not ...').
segments.s750-g296: دل و پشت ایرانیان is a coordinated object phrase 'the heart and the back of the Iranians' (ایرانیان = genitive/possessive group). نشکنی is a 2nd-person singular negative/subjunctive form of شکستن used after تا to express prohibition or warning ('that you do not break'). Grammatically, the verb governs the coordinated NP and gives the clause its prohibitory content.
segments.s750-g225: هران کس که ما را بدی زیردست is an older phrasing meaning “whoever/anyone who …”; هران کس که = “whomever” (هر + آن کس). ما را is the object marked by را. زیردست functions as a predicate noun meaning “(his) underling/subordinate.” بدی here is a nominal/qualifying word (“badness / doing ill”) that links the object to its state — roughly “made us (into) bad underlings” in context.
segments.s750-g226: چنین باژها بر هیونان مست literally lists what follows from that situation: چنین = “such/like this”; باژها (plural) = “cries/noises”; بر + هیونان = “upon the beasts” (بر = on/upon); مست qualifies هیونان as “drunk” or figuratively wildly intoxicated. The noun phrase describes the sound the beasts made.
segments.s750-g227: همی‌تاختند is a past, repeated/continuous action formed with the old prefix همی- + past stem تاختند = “they charged/rushed repeatedly.” به درگاه ما = “toward/into our court/threshold,” where درگاه means the royal court or audience-hall and ما is the possessive/object marker “our.”
segments.s750-g228: نپیچید is negated past (ن‑ + پیچید) meaning “did not turn.” گردن کس = “anyone’s neck” (گردن = neck) is topicalized before the verb; از راه ما = “from/off our way/path.” The clause literally says “no one’s neck turned from our way,” i.e. nobody turned aside from our path.
segments.s750-g229: ز here is the poetic form of از = “from.” هر در = “each/ every gate/door” (در in the sense ‘gate’). فراوان is an adverb/adjective “abundantly/much” modifying کشیدیم رنج. کشیدیم is first-person plural past of کشیدن; with رنج it means “we suffered/endured hardship.” Word order is typical: prepositional phrase + adverb + verb + object.
segments.s750-g230: بدان is a compound usage (ب‑ + دان/آن) giving a directional/result sense “to/for that,” followed by تا which introduces the result/purpose clause. بیاگند is a prefixed present/subjunctive-like form (بی‑ + آگند) meaning “may rot / come to rot.” زین گونه گنج = “of this kind of treasure” where زین = “on/at the saddle” (literal saddle/trappings here) or a determiner-like form, گونه = “kind/type,” and گنج = “treasure.” Overall: “...so that this sort of treasure would rot on the saddle.”
segments.s750-g231: دگر here = “other/else/then.” The two noun phrases are appositive treasures: گنج خضرا = “the green/Khazra treasure” (خضرا likely a proper name/personification here) and گنج عروس = “the bride’s treasure” or a named hoard; و links them. The noun‑noun constructions place the modifier after گنج (treasure + epithet/name).
segments.s750-g232: کجا داشتیم is an inverted interrogative (کجا = “where” placed before the past-tense داشتیم “we had/possessed”), producing “where did we have …?” از پی = “after / following”; روز بوس = “the day(s) of kissing” (روز = day, بوس = kiss). The prepositional phrase modifies the time reference for the question.
segments.s750-g81: Literal, somewhat elliptical poetic order: یکی (a certain person) is the subject; کاخ بد کرده is a past-action phrase (‘had built/made a palace’, where کرده is the verbal perfect/participle). زندانش نام is an inverted way of saying ‘called it his prison’ (نام as ‘name’ with an implied verbal idea ‘to call’ — the verb is omitted for poetic compression).
segments.s750-g82: همی + زیستی is an archaic/habitual past sense (here ‘used to live’ / ‘lived’) with همی giving continuous or habitual aspect; اندرو is the locative ‘in/therein’; شادکام is a predicate adjective ‘happy/fortunate’. Together: ‘therein he lived happily.’
segments.s750-g83: همان نیز modifies گفتار اخترشناس: ‘that very / the same speech of the astrologer’ with نیز ‘also’ adding an inclusive/emphatic sense. The noun phrase order is the common poetic ordering (demonstrative + particle + noun + modifier).
segments.s750-g84: که introduces reported content (‘that …’). ما را is the patient/object ‘us’ (را marks definiteness/emphasis); همی is habitual/continuous (‘always’); از تو places the source/agent (‘because of / from you’); دادی is 2nd-person past ‘you gave/caused’ (here ‘you cause’); هراس is the object ‘fear’. The whole clause reports the astrologer’s words: ‘that you always cause us fear.’
segments.s750-g85: که again introduces a content/relative clause. از تو = ‘from you’ (source). بد آید uses بد (evil/bad) + آید (come/happen); together ‘evil/misfortune may come’. بدین سان is a fixed adverbial phrase ‘in this way / in this very manner’ (بد- as prefix + دین سان forming بدین‌سان); که هست is a relative/additional clause ‘as it is’ or ‘just as it is now’. Overall: ‘that misfortune might come from you in this very way.’
segments.s750-g86: نینداختم is 1st-person past negative of انداختن (‘I did not cast/throw’), here used figuratively. اخترت is ‘your star’ (اختر + ـت possessive), را marks the object, and ز دست = ‘out of (my) hand/power’ — an idiom meaning ‘out of my control’. Together: ‘I did not cast your star out of my hand’ = ‘I did not let your fate slip from my control.’
segments.s750-g87: وزان پس links back to the previous result (‘and/from that, then’). نهادیم is 1st-person plural past ‘we placed/set’. مهری here functions as ‘a token of affection’ (or ‘a mark/pledge of love’); به روی literally ‘on the face’ — a phrase meaning publicly or on the cheek/forehead in poetic usage. The line describes placing a love-token or mark.
segments.s750-g88: به شیرین سپردیم = ‘we entrusted to Shirin’ (به + name as indirect object; سپردیم = ‘we handed/entrusted’). زان گفت و گوی = ‘that talk/conversation’ (زان = ‘from/that;’ گفت و گو is the classical paired words meaning ‘conversation’ or ‘that matter of speech’). So: ‘we entrusted that matter/conversation to Shirin.’
segments.s750-g321: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause; قیصر is the subject ('the Caesar/emperor'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from' governing گرد بلا ('the dust of calamity'), where گرد = 'dust' and بلا = 'calamity'; بشست is a past-tense verb 'washed' agreeing with قیصر, so the whole segment means 'when the Caesar washed his face from the dust of calamity.'
segments.s750-g322: به here marks direction/target 'toward' (he sprang toward someone); مردی is an indefinite 'a man'; چو is the comparative 'like' linking the following name as a simile; پرویز is the proper name used as a simile; داماد is an epithet 'the groom / son‑in‑law' modifying پرویز; جست is the simple past 'sprang/leapt.' The clause reads 'a man like Parviz the groom leapt toward (him).'
segments.s750-g323: هر آنکس که is a relative phrase 'whoever / every person who'; گیتی is the direct object 'the world'; به + بد here means 'to/into bad (hands)' — 'to evil'; نسپرد is the past negative of سپردن 'did not entrust/hand over.' So this clause says 'whoever did not entrust the world to evil.'
segments.s750-g324: به + مغز اندرون is an adverbial phrase 'in/within the inward brain' (مغز = 'brain, mind', اندرون = 'inside'); باشد is the copular/existential form here ('is / will be'); او را خرد places او as the possessor/topic marked by را and خرد is 'wisdom.' Word order is typical Persian: locative phrase + copula + possessor phrase, yielding 'wisdom is within his inner mind.'
segments.s750-g325: بدانم is the first‑person present/factual 'I know'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'; بهرام is the subject; بسته is a passive/participial adjective 'bound/closed/tied'; میان is a locative 'in the middle / among.' Together they say 'I know that Bahram is bound/locked in the middle.'
segments.s750-g326: ابا here is used as 'refusal/reluctance' (a noun); او is the possessor/agent 'his'; یکی گشته is a perfective/passive construction 'have become one / united' (گشته = 'become'); ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians.' The sequence literally reads 'his refusal — the Iranians have become one,' i.e. 'because of his refusal the Iranians became united.'
segments.s750-g327: به رومی سپاهی places رومی ('the Roman / of Rûm') with سپاهی ('army/host') in a prepositional/agentive phrase (به … سپاهی can be read 'for the Roman an army' or 'an army against the Romans'); نشاید is the impersonal/modal 'it is not fitting / should not be'; شکست is a verbal noun 'defeat' used as the complement. So the clause means roughly 'It is not fitting that an army be defeated by the Romans.'
segments.s750-g328: نساید is a negative present-tense verb from ساییدن 'to wear/abrade' ('does not wear away'); روان is the patient 'soul/spirit' (here the thing threatened); ریگ + با + کوه دست is an instrumental/comparative cluster 'like sand with the hand of the mountain' (ریگ = 'sand', با = 'with', کوه دست = 'the mountain's hand / force'). Grammatically, the verb governs روان and the following phrase gives the comparison/instrument: 'the spirit will not be ground away like sand by the mountain's hand.'
segments.s750-g393: 'که' introduces the clause ('that' / 'so that') and marks the following phrase as a wished-for statement. 'پدرود' is a noun ('farewell') serving as the theme of the wish. 'بادی' is an adjectival/qualifying word here ('windy; wind-like'), so the phrase says the farewell should be like a wind. 'تو' is the beneficiary ('to you'). 'تا' = 'until' and 'جاودان' = 'eternal', together 'until eternity'. The whole segment functions as one optative clause: 'that your farewell be wind-like to you until eternity.'
segments.s750-g394: 'سر و کار' is an idiomatic compound ('head and work') meaning 'affairs' or 'concerns'; the possessor 'ما' follows the compound as in classical Persian (noun + possessor). 'باد' here is the optative/particle meaning 'may it be' (not the noun 'wind'), giving the clause a wish: 'may our affairs be...'. 'با' introduces the complement 'with' and 'بخردان' is the plural agent ('the wise/prudent'). Grammatically this is a separate optative clause coordinated with the previous wish.
segments.s750-g395: 'شما‌ای' is an emphatic or vocative form of 'you' (the pronoun plus the vocative clitic -ای), addressing the addressees directly. 'گرامی' is an epithet ('honored, dear') modifying the following noun. 'فرستادگان' is the head noun in plural ('envoys, messengers'). Together this is a vocative phrase: 'you, honored envoys.'
segments.s750-g396: 'سخن گوی' is a nominal compound: 'سخن' ('speech') + 'گوی' (here functioning like 'speaker' / 'speaking'), meaning 'eloquent' or 'speakers of speech'. The coordinating 'و' links the next descriptive phrase. 'پر' here is the preposed adjective 'full (of)', modifying 'مایه' ('substance, resource'); 'پر مایه' = 'rich in substance' or 'well-resourced'. 'آزادگان' is the group being described ('the free / freeborn'). The whole segment is a string of epithets describing the envoys: eloquent and substance-rich among the free.
segments.s750-g397: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'من' is the source ('me' = 'from me'). 'هر' here (in 'هر دو') serves to mark 'both' (each of the two). 'پدرود' again means 'farewell' (the object/content of the wish). 'باشید' is the second-person plural optative/imperative of 'to be' ('be' / 'may you be'), directing the envoys. 'نیز' = 'also / likewise' ties this injunction to what was just said. The clause is an imperative/optative instruction: 'be both farewells from me, too.'
segments.s750-g398: Word order is fronted for emphasis: 'سخن' ('speech') appears at the start, followed by the limiting 'جز' ('except') and the modifier 'شنیده' ('heard' — past participle). 'مگویید' is the negative imperative ('do not say' addressed to plural 'you'), and 'چیز' ('thing') is a final generalizing word ('anything/thing'). Together: 'Say nothing except the heard speech' → 'Do not say anything except what you have heard.'
segments.s750-g399: 'کنم' is first-person singular present/future of 'to do' ('I do / I make'), here introducing the speaker's action. 'آفرین' is the object ('praise, acclamation') — 'I give praise'. 'بر' is the preposition 'upon/for', and 'جهان' is 'the world'. 'سر به سر' is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'throughout' or 'completely' (the two 'سر' instances form the adverbial phrase). The sequence is literal: 'I make acclamation upon the world, all through.'
segments.s750-g400: 'که' here introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for / because / that'). 'او' is the third-person pronoun ('him/her'); 'را' is the Persian definite object marker attached to the pronoun. 'ندیدم' is the negative past ('I did not see'). 'مگر' = 'except', and 'بر گذر' ('on passing') is a short prepositional phrase meaning 'in passing' or 'on the way'. Together: 'for I did not see him except in passing.'
segments.s750-g241: نماند is the verb ماندن with the negative prefix ن- (lit. 'does not remain' / 'there remains none') placed before its logical subject کس; the clause order is verb → subject → locative phrase, so the literal reading is 'remains no one in the world (of) tranquility.' اندر جهان رامشی is a prepositional locative ('in the world') plus رامشی as the state 'tranquility' that no one retains.
segments.s750-g242: نباید is an impersonal modal negative ('one should not' / 'it is not proper'), followed by the infinitive گزیدن ('to choose'); بَجز introduces the exception phrase. The whole fragment means 'one must not choose except silence' (i.e. one should choose nothing but silence).
segments.s750-g243: همی‌کرد is an archaic/imperfective verbal form 'would do' (habitual/conditional), combined with خواهی ('if you wish' / 'if you want') to form a conditional sense; the predicate جهان پر گزند ('the world full of harm') follows, so the clause reads 'if you would/should do that, the world will be/ be filled with harm.'
segments.s750-g244: پر از is the fixed construction 'full of' + a noun phrase; here درد is 'affliction/pain' and کاری modifies that pain (an active/agentive sense intended, not the agricultural senses listed), followed by و ناسودمند 'and unprofitable.' The line characterizes the fullness as painful and fruitless labour — 'full of painful, unprofitable (things).'
segments.s750-g245: همان is the demonstrative 'that/that very,' followed by پر گزندان 'full/heap of harms' (گزندان is a poetic/collective form of گزند). که introduces a relative clause; نزد توانَد means 'can be near/at the presence (of someone)' — here indicating harms that can come close or be present.
segments.s750-g246: The second که again introduces a relative or explanatory clause; تیره modifies شبان as 'dark' (here 'تیره شبان' = 'dark nights' in poetic usage). اورمزد (Ohrmazd/Ahura Mazda) plus تواند ('can') states that even the god (Ohrmazd) is involved/able in what the dark nights do — a poetic way to emphasize the severity of those nights. Note: شبان is a poetic/archaic form meaning 'nights' rather than the literal senses listed in the word table.
segments.s750-g247: همی here is an emphatic/archaic particle accompanying the verb phrase; the verb appears in inverted order 'همی داد خواهند' (archaic inversion of خواهند داد) meaning 'they will give' or idiomatically 'they will cast/throw.' تختت is 'your throne' (تخت + enclitic -ت), and به باد is an idiom 'to the wind' meaning to ruin or scatter. Overall: 'They will surely cast your throne to the winds.'
segments.s750-g248: بدان is the imperative 'know this' composed of the prefix ب- + دان 'know.' تا introduces a result/purpose clause ('so that'), and نباشی is a negative subjunctive/imperative form 'you may not be / do not be.' به گیتی تو شاد is 'happy in the world, you.' Put together: 'Know this so that you will not be glad in the world.'
segments.s750-g417: A short nominal phrase describing the subject. همه 'the whole/entire' qualifies the following location phrase; در + خوشاب places that 'glow/complexion' on the body (خوشاب here denotes a pleasant, rosy hue). پیکرش is the possessive noun 'his body/figure' (پیکر + enclitic -ش). Word order is adjective/adverbial (در خوشاب) before the noun it describes.
segments.s750-g418: A verbless predicate clause turned into a predicate by the past copula بودی. ز is the poetic form of از ('from'/'of'), attaching to یاقوت 'ruby' which with the adjective رخشنده 'radiant' forms the predicate; بودی is past tense 'was'. درش = در + ش is a locative 'in it' with the 3rd‑person enclitic referring back to پیکرش.
segments.s750-g419: A nominal apposition: the name سیاوش is immediately specified by همان 'that' + نامدار 'renowned', followed by the appositive name هژیر. همان functions as a demonstrative intensifier ('that very / the famed').
segments.s750-g420: A relative clause introduced by که 'who/whom'. کشتش is کشت + object clitic -ش 'killed him' (کشت here = 'killing'); به‌روز جوانی is a temporal phrase 'in the day/prime of youth' (به‌روز = 'on the day/of the time'), and دبیر is the agent noun 'the scribe/scholar' placed after the temporal phrase (poetic word order) to indicate who performed the killing.
segments.s750-g421: An interrogative clause beginning with کجا 'where'. گنگ modifies دژ as an adjective 'mute/silent fortress'. کرد is the past verb 'made/raised'; جایی is an adverb 'somewhere/anywhere'; به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'by/with hardship' specifying the manner in which the fortress was raised. The verb precedes the place adjuncts in this poetic inversion.
segments.s750-g422: وزان = و + زان ('and from that' / 'and of that'), followed by رنج برده where برده is a past participle/derived form meaning 'having borne' or 'taken' (here 'having endured that hardship'). ندید is the past 'saw not' and ایچ 'nothing/at all' negates گنج 'treasure' — together 'saw no treasure at all.'
segments.s750-g423: An interrogative phrase introduced by کجا 'where' followed by a triad of proper names (رستم زال و اسفندیار). The list is in apposition to the interrogative 'where are X', i.e., asking for the whereabouts or fate of these famous heroes.
segments.s750-g424: کزیشان = کز + ایشان functions as 'from/of them' (a relative/ablative link). سخن 'speech/tale' is the theme that 'ماندمان' (literally 'that which remained to us' or 'our remnant') marks as what endures, and یادگار 'memento/memorial' characterizes that remainder. The phrase means 'from them a tale remains to us as a memorial.'
segments.s750-g177: This clause means 'whatever they say about me from now on (will be so)'. ز من ('from/about me') uses ز in the poetic sense ‘from, concerning’; هرچ گویند = 'whatever they say' with گویند functioning as a 3rd-person plural present verb 'they say' (classical/poetic word order places the verb after its phrase). زین پس = 'from now on' and همان is a verblike predicative 'the same / so be it' that ties to the next verb (شوند) in the sentence.
segments.s750-g178: شوند is the 3rd-person plural of شدن 'become / be' (‘they/those things become / hold true’). این گره 'this knot' (metaphorical problem/trouble) is the subject, بر تو = 'upon you' with بر 'on/upon', and بر بد گمان is an ablative/circumstantial phrase 'because of ill/bad suspicion' (بر + compound adjective; word order places the cause after the affected person).
segments.s750-g179: همه بنده سیم و زرند و بس reads as a general statement: 'all (همه) are slaves (بنده) to silver and gold (سیم و زر) — and that's enough (بس)'. The copula is omitted (typical in verse); the final بس is a closing/exhaustive particle 'enough/that is all.'
segments.s750-g180: کسی را نباشند فریادرس = 'there will be no one who is a rescuer.' کسی را uses را as a marker giving an indefinite/affected sense ('anyone'); نباشند is the negative form of بودن in plural/impersonal sense 'there are not / there will be no', and فریادرس is the predicate noun 'rescuer/succour'.
segments.s750-g181: ازیشان (از ایشان) 'because of/from them' gives the cause; تو را uses را to mark the affected person ('to you' / topicalized 'you'), دل پر آسایش است = 'the heart is full of comfort/peace' where پر is the postpositive adjective 'full (of)'. The copula است completes the predication.
segments.s750-g182: گناه مرا جای پالایش است = 'my sin is a place of purification.' Here گناه 'sin' is possessed by مرا (tokenization shows م + را; grammatically this is 'مرا' = 'my'/'me' as possessor in this line), جای 'a place' governs the noun پالایش 'purification', and است is the copula. The construction treats the speaker's sin as serving the function of purification.
segments.s750-g183: نگنجد is the negative present of گنجیدن 'to be contained / fit' ('does not fit'). تو را marks the experiencer/indirect object ('for you' / topicalized), این سخن 'this speech/remark' is the subject, and در خرد = 'in (your) reason/mind' places the content in the intellect. The order is typical poetic inversion (verb before subject pronoun marked by را).
segments.s750-g184: This clause negates a causal explanation: 'نه زین بد که گفتی کسی برخورد' — نه introduces negation; زین بد 'this evil/ill thing' (zīn = archaic demonstrative 'this') is the supposed cause; که introduces the relative/clause 'that you said' (گفتی 'you said' — a reported speech marker), and کسی برخورد 'someone encountered/ran into it' (برخورد as 'encounter'). The overall sense: 'it is not because of that bad thing you spoke of that anyone encountered it.'
segments.s750-g193: ببردیم is a first‑person plural past form ('we ...') here meaning a completed martial action; it takes the following infinitival/nominal clause as its object. بر is the preposition 'against' (classical use before a plural noun). دشمنان is the plural direct object 'the enemies'. تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to charge, to make an attack' and here names the action that was carried out against the enemies.
segments.s750-g194: نیارست is an older/poetic negative/potential verb meaning 'it was not possible / could not' (used impersonally here) — the classical negative + ability sense; it governs کس in the sense 'anyone/no one'. کس here with the negative means 'no one'. گردن افراختن is a common idiom: گردن (neck) + the light verb افراختن 'to raise' — together 'to raise the neck', i.e. 'to lift one's head' or 'to show pride'.
segments.s750-g195: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic). دشمن is 'the enemy' (subject). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here 'out of/from the'). گیتی means 'the world/earth'. پراگنده شد is an intransitive perfective phrase 'was scattered' or 'became scattered' (پراگنده = scattered + شد as the past/resultive auxiliary).
segments.s750-g196: همه = 'all' (here 'all of' or 'the whole of'). گنج 'treasure' is the noun. ما is the genitive/possessive 'our'. یک سر (one‑head) functions idiomatically as 'completely/at once' (old compound use). آگنده is a predicative adjective 'filled/overflowing' and شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was'. Word order is subject–object–predicate typical of classical Persian lines: 'all our treasure (subject) became completely filled.'
segments.s750-g197: همه بوم شد = 'the whole bōm (land/homeland) became' — بوم = 'land, homeland' and شد is the past 'became'. نزد ما is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for us / with respect to us' (نزد here signals beneficiary/interest). کارگر is a predicate adjective 'effective, useful, productive' rather than the modern 'worker' — so the clause means 'the whole land proved useful to us.'
segments.s750-g198: ز دریا = 'from the sea' (ز = from). کشیدند is a past plural verb 'they pulled/drew' (here physical drawing up of pearls/jewels). چندان is an adverb 'so much/so many' or 'to that extent' modifying گهر. گهر = 'gems, jewels'. The clause is active: 'From the sea they drew so many jewels.'
